proposals.title: Enhancing the durability of fire-retardant wood coatings through plasma pre-treatment and sol-gel nanolayer deposition
proposals.motivation: Fire-retardant (FR) finishes for timber cladding are crucial for building safety but suffer from poor durability against weathering and moisture sorption, leading to frequent reapplication and waste. Pre-2026 research by Hill and others highlights the hygroscopic challenges of modified wood and the degradation of archaeological wood, underscoring the need for finishes that protect the wood substrate without compromising long-term performance. Applying surface science to create durable, multi-functional FR systems addresses a key gap in sustainable construction materials.
proposals.hypothesis: That a two-step surface engineering approach—comprising atmospheric plasma etching to increase surface energy and adhesion, followed by deposition of a hybrid organic-inorganic sol-gel nanocoating doped with FR compounds—will create a more durable and moisture-resistant FR finish for wood, significantly reducing leaching and weathering degradation compared to conventional treatments.
proposals.method: Thermally modified Scots pine samples will be pre-treated using a dielectric barrier discharge atmospheric plasma system to functionalize the wood surface. A sol-gel precursor solution of tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS) and 3-glycidyloxypropyltrimethoxysilane (GPTMS), doped with a commercial phosphorus-nitrogen FR agent, will be applied via dip-coating to form a hybrid silica network. Coating durability will be assessed through artificial weathering (EN 927-6), water immersion tests, and dynamic vapor sorption (DVS) analysis to monitor moisture behavior. Leaching resistance will be quantified via ICP-OES analysis of run-off water.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel methodology for creating durable, multi-functional wood coatings that combine fire retardancy with enhanced moisture resistance. It will contribute fundamental data on the moisture sorption behavior of plasma-modified wood with nanocoatings, bridging the fields of wood modification, conservation science, and materials surface engineering. The results will inform more sustainable maintenance strategies for external timber cladding.
proposals.novelty: While plasma treatment and sol-gel coatings have been studied separately, their combined, sequential application specifically to enhance FR finish durability on wood is unexplored. This approach integrates pre-2026 knowledge from wood conservation (barrier layers) and thermal modification (substrate stability) to solve a contemporary problem in building material performance.

proposals.title: Hygro-Mechanical Performance of Wood-Based Hybrid Composites with Corrosion-Resistant Steel and Carbon Fibres for External Cladding
proposals.motivation: The use of modified wood in external cladding is limited by its susceptibility to moisture-induced dimensional instability and long-term degradation, impacting service life. While synthetic fibre composites offer high performance, their cost and environmental footprint are concerns. A hybrid composite system, leveraging the ductility and cost-effectiveness of steel fibres with the strength of synthetic fibres in a wood matrix, could provide a tailored, durable, and more sustainable material for demanding built environments.
proposals.hypothesis: That incorporating a hybrid reinforcement of corrosion-resistant stainless steel microfibres and discontinuous carbon fibres into a thermally modified wood or densified wood matrix will yield a composite with superior and more predictable hygro-mechanical properties (reduced moisture sorption, enhanced dimensional stability, and tailored stiffness-toughness balance) compared to mono-fibre composites or unmodified wood.
proposals.method: Thermally modified spruce (TMW) and densified delignified wood (DDW) will serve as matrices. Composites will be fabricated via resin impregnation and pressing, with hybrid reinforcements of austenitic stainless steel (AISI 316L) fibres and short carbon fibres at controlled ratios (e.g., 75:25, 50:50, 25:75 by volume). Key tests include dynamic vapour sorption (DVS) analysis to model moisture behaviour, mechanical 3-point bending with digital image correlation (DIC) for strain mapping, and accelerated weathering cycles. Microstructural analysis will use SEM/EDS to assess fibre-matrix adhesion and dispersion.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel material design framework for next-generation bio-based cladding, directly linking hybrid fibre architecture to hygro-mechanical performance. It will provide quantitative data on the life-cycle moisture behaviour and mechanical tailoring of wood-hybrid composites, bridging the gap between wood science, archaeological conservation (understanding composite degradation), and advanced materials engineering.
proposals.novelty: The application of a steel-synthetic fibre hybrid system within a modified wood matrix is unexplored, moving beyond polymer matrices. The research uniquely integrates principles from wood moisture sorption science and conservation material studies to engineer a composite where moisture management is a core design parameter, not just an add-on property.

proposals.title: Re-evaluating water vapour sorption in modified and archaeological wood: a comparative analysis of Hailwood-Horrobin, GAB, and Dent models
proposals.motivation: The Hailwood-Horrobin (H-H) model is the dominant framework for interpreting sorption isotherms in wood science, particularly for deriving the 'monolayer moisture content' used to assess dimensional stability and degradation. However, its universal application to non-standard materials like thermally modified or waterlogged archaeological wood is largely assumed, not critically validated. A rigorous comparison with other established physical sorption models (e.g., GAB, Dent) is needed to determine if the H-H model provides the most accurate description or if alternatives offer superior fits and more meaningful physical parameters for these complex materials.
proposals.hypothesis: For thermally modified and waterlogged archaeological wood, sorption models such as the Guggenheim-Anderson-de Boer (GAB) and Dent models will provide statistically better fits to experimental sorption data than the Hailwood-Horrobin model, leading to significantly different calculated monolayer values and improved interpretation of cell wall-water interactions.
proposals.method: Sorption isotherms (adsorption and desorption) will be generated at 20°C for a matrix of wood samples: untreated reference species, thermally modified wood (at different intensities), and conserved archaeological wood. Isotherm data will be fitted to the H-H, GAB, and Dent models using non-linear least squares regression (e.g., Levenberg-Marquardt algorithm). Model performance will be evaluated using Akaike Information Criterion (AIC), adjusted R², and analysis of residuals. The derived monolayer values and other model parameters (e.g., the GAB 'k' constant) will be statistically compared across treatments and models.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a critical, evidence-based assessment of sorption model validity for modified and historical wood, moving beyond tradition. It will establish whether the monolayer moisture content—a key indicator of cell wall accessibility and stability—is model-dependent for these materials. The findings will directly inform best practices in wood modification research and the conservation science of waterlogged artefacts.
proposals.novelty: While these models are known in food and polymer science, a systematic comparative test against the entrenched H-H model in wood science, specifically for non-standard wood materials central to Hill's research themes, is novel. It challenges a foundational assumption in the field's methodology.

proposals.title: Comparative Analysis of Moisture Sorption Hysteresis in Non-Wood Natural Fibers: Implications for Biocomposite Durability and Conservation
proposals.motivation: The hygroscopic behavior of wood is well-studied, but there is a significant knowledge gap regarding the sorption properties of other commercially relevant natural fibers (e.g., sisal, ramie, bamboo) used in modern biocomposites and found in archaeological contexts. Understanding their moisture sorption hysteresis is critical for predicting dimensional stability, mechanical performance, and long-term durability in built environments, as well as for informing conservation strategies for waterlogged organic artifacts beyond wood.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the magnitude and characteristics of sorption hysteresis in lignocellulosic fibers like sisal, ramie, and bamboo differ significantly from those of solid wood due to variations in their chemical composition (hemicellulose type and content, lignin structure) and ultra-structural morphology, leading to distinct moisture durability profiles.
proposals.method: The study will employ dynamic vapour sorption (DVS) analysis to generate full adsorption and desorption isotherms at 25°C for selected fibers (sisal, ramie, bamboo, with spruce as a wood reference). Hysteresis will be quantified by calculating the area between isotherms. Complementary spectroscopic (FTIR) and chemical (Klason lignin, holocellulose) analyses will correlate hysteresis behavior with fiber composition. The Guggenheim-Anderson-de Boer (GAB) model will be fitted to the isotherm data to derive monolayer moisture content and binding energy parameters for comparative analysis.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic dataset on sorption hysteresis for these under-characterized fibers, directly informing material selection for moisture-sensitive biocomposite applications. It will extend the principles of wood modification and conservation science to a broader class of bio-materials, offering a predictive framework for their hygroscopic performance and degradation risks in varying climates.
proposals.novelty: While sorption in wood is extensively documented, a direct comparative study of hysteresis in these specific non-wood fibers, linking it to their distinct biochemistry for both engineering and conservation outcomes, represents a novel synthesis of the researcher's expertise in timber modification and archaeological wood science.

proposals.title: A Predictive Model for Sorption Hysteresis in Wood Based on Lignin Content and Cell Wall Architecture
proposals.motivation: Sorption hysteresis significantly influences the dimensional stability, durability, and long-term performance of wood in construction and conservation. While lignin is known to be a hydrophobic, non-swellable polymer that restricts moisture access to hemicelluloses, a quantitative, predictive relationship between lignin content and hysteresis magnitude remains elusive. Establishing such a relationship would advance fundamental understanding of wood-moisture interactions and provide a material science basis for optimizing wood modification treatments and interpreting the moisture behaviour of archaeological wood.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the area of the sorption hysteresis loop in wood can be quantitatively predicted by a non-linear function of lignin content, modulated by the accessibility of the cell wall matrix, which is influenced by thermal modification and degradation state.
proposals.method: We will use dynamic vapour sorption (DVS) to generate full adsorption-desorption isotherms (0-95% RH) for a controlled sample set: (i) native wood species with varying natural lignin content, (ii) thermally modified wood (TMW) with known lignin enrichment, and (iii) artificially degraded wood simulating archaeological states. Lignin content will be quantified using the Klason method. Hysteresis loop area will be calculated by numerical integration (e.g., trapezoidal rule). The relationship will be modelled using multivariate regression and non-linear fitting (e.g., power-law or exponential decay functions), with lignin content and a measure of matrix accessibility (from nitrogen porosimetry or solute exclusion) as independent variables.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first mathematical model directly correlating a key chemical constituent with a critical hygroscopic property. It will provide a tool for predicting the moisture performance of modified wood products and offer a new diagnostic metric for assessing the state of degradation in waterlogged archaeological wood, linking conservation science to material science principles.
proposals.novelty: While lignin's role in reducing equilibrium moisture content is known, quantitatively linking it to the hysteresis *area*—a dynamic, path-dependent property—is novel. The integrated study of native, modified, and degraded wood within a single predictive framework bridges the gap between contemporary timber engineering and archaeological wood conservation.

proposals.title: Simultaneous In-Situ Measurement of Moisture Sorption and Viscoelastic Properties of Modified Wood via Coupled Dynamic Vapor Sorption and Dynamic Mechanical Analysis
proposals.motivation: Understanding the moisture-mechanics relationship is critical for predicting the service life and dimensional stability of wood in construction and for conserving archaeological wood. Current methods rely on separate, sequential measurements of sorption isotherms and mechanical properties, creating a temporal disconnect that fails to capture the real-time, hysteretic effects of moisture cycling on material performance. This gap limits the accuracy of models for wood behaviour in variable climates.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesise that coupling DVS and DMA will reveal a direct, non-linear relationship between instantaneous moisture content and the viscoelastic properties (e.g., storage modulus, loss modulus) of wood, and that this relationship will be fundamentally altered by thermal modification, providing a quantitative basis for its enhanced stability.
proposals.method: We will develop a novel experimental setup by integrating a commercial Dynamic Vapor Sorption (DVS) system with a Dynamic Mechanical Analysis (DMA) instrument in tensile or three-point bending mode. Small, thin wood specimens (including thermally modified and unmodified controls) will be subjected to precisely controlled relative humidity ramps (e.g., 0-95% RH) within the DVS chamber while the DMA simultaneously applies a small oscillatory strain. The DMA will measure the complex modulus in real-time, with mass change recorded concurrently by the DVS microbalance. Data will be synchronised via custom software to correlate moisture content (MC) directly with mechanical property changes.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first continuous, in-situ dataset linking hygroscopic and viscoelastic behaviour of wood, creating a foundational model for moisture-stress coupling. It will deliver quantitative metrics for how thermal modification decouples moisture uptake from mechanical softening, directly informing material selection for building cladding and predictive models for the conservation of waterlogged wooden artefacts.
proposals.novelty: While DVS and DMA are established techniques, their synchronous, coupled application to wood science is novel. This approach moves beyond correlative studies to establish direct causation, capturing the dynamic mechanical response to sorption hysteresis in a single experiment, which has not been achieved pre-2026.

proposals.title: Bridging the Scale Gap: Predicting Moisture Sorption in Wood-Polymer Composites from the Hygroscopic Behaviour of Modified Fibres
proposals.motivation: The long-term performance and durability of wood-polymer composites (WPCs) in construction and cladding are critically dependent on their moisture sorption behaviour, which can lead to dimensional instability and decay. While the sorption properties of solid wood and modified timber are well-studied, and the conservation of waterlogged wood informs on extreme moisture states, a fundamental gap exists in predicting composite-scale sorption from the properties of its constituent fibres. This limits the rational design of WPCs with controlled hygroscopicity for specific environments.
proposals.hypothesis: The equilibrium moisture content (EMC) of a wood-polymer composite is not a simple linear function of its fibre content, but can be predicted by a model that integrates the sorption isotherms of the individual fibres (modified or unmodified) with the composite's microstructure and the polymer matrix's barrier properties.
proposals.method: We will first characterize the sorption isotherms (at 20°C, 0-95% RH) of individual wood fibres from thermally modified and unmodified timber using dynamic vapour sorption (DVS) analysis. These fibres will then be compounded with a polypropylene matrix at controlled loadings (20-60 wt%) and orientations to manufacture composite samples. The composite sorption performance will be measured via gravimetric analysis under identical climatic cycles. A micromechanical model, based on pre-2026 composite theory (e.g., Halpin-Tsai equations adapted for hygro-expansion) and informed by SEM analysis of fibre dispersion and interface quality, will be developed to predict composite EMC from fibre isotherm data.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a predictive framework for designing WPCs with tailored moisture response, directly linking fibre-level modification chemistry to bulk composite performance. It will offer new insights for the development of durable bio-based cladding materials and inform conservation science on the behaviour of composite artefacts. The dataset will be a valuable benchmark for future lifecycle assessments of such materials.
proposals.novelty: While sorption in solid wood and polymers is studied separately, this research uniquely focuses on the scaling challenge, applying principles from archaeological wood conservation (multi-scale moisture behaviour) and thermal modification research to the engineered composite space, which has been largely empirical regarding moisture uptake prediction.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Long-Term Impact Resistance of Modified and Archaeological Wood Under Cyclic Hygrothermal Aging
proposals.motivation: The long-term performance of wood in external cladding and structural applications is critically dependent on its resistance to impact damage, which can initiate decay. While thermal modification is known to alter hygroscopicity, and archaeological studies reveal wood's degradation over centuries, there is a lack of quantitative data linking controlled environmental aging (moisture and temperature cycles) to the evolution of impact properties in modern modified timbers. This gap hinders predictive service life modeling and the development of more durable wood products.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that cyclic hygrothermal aging will lead to a quantifiable reduction in the impact resistance of wood, and that the rate and extent of this reduction will differ significantly between untreated, thermally modified, and chemically modified wood, correlating with changes in their moisture sorption behavior and chemical composition.
proposals.method: Specimens of Scots pine (Pinus sylvestris) and European oak (Quercus robur) will be prepared in untreated, thermally modified (ThermoWood process), and dimethyloldihydroxyethyleneurea (DMDHEU)-modified states. A subset will undergo artificial accelerated aging in climate chambers applying cyclic variations in relative humidity (30-90%) and temperature (10-50°C) over 12 months. Impact resistance will be quantified pre- and post-aging using a Charpy impact test (ISO 179-1). Parallel analysis will include dynamic vapour sorption (DVS) to monitor hysteresis changes, FTIR spectroscopy to track chemical alterations, and microscopy to assess micro-crack formation. Data will be analyzed using analysis of variance (ANOVA) and regression models to correlate environmental exposure with mechanical decline.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first dataset directly linking hygrothermal aging cycles to the degradation of impact properties in modified wood, creating a foundation for more accurate service life predictions. It will bridge the fields of wood modification, building physics, and archaeological conservation by applying accelerated aging methodologies to understand long-term behavior, ultimately informing material selection and preservation strategies.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the targeted application of impact testing—a property highly sensitive to micro-damage—to quantify the effects of precisely controlled hygrothermal aging on modern modified woods, creating a direct mechanistic link between sorption behavior, chemical change, and a key durability metric not commonly studied in this context.

proposals.title: Enhancing Flax Fiber-Matrix Adhesion in Biocomposites: A Transfer of Chemical Modification Techniques from Wood Fiber Treatments
proposals.motivation: The development of sustainable, high-performance biocomposites using natural fibers like flax is critical for reducing the environmental impact of the materials sector. However, the poor interfacial adhesion between hydrophilic flax fibers and hydrophobic polymer matrices remains a major limitation, compromising mechanical properties and long-term durability. While extensive research exists on chemical treatments (e.g., acetylation, silanization, alkali treatment) to improve wood fiber-matrix adhesion in wood-plastic composites, their systematic application and optimization for flax fibers are underexplored.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that chemical modification techniques proven effective for wood fibers can be successfully transferred to flax fibers to significantly enhance their interfacial adhesion with common polymer matrices (e.g., polypropylene, polylactic acid), leading to improved mechanical performance and moisture sorption stability of the resulting biocomposites.
proposals.method: Flax fibers will be treated with three established wood fiber modification methods: alkaline (NaOH) treatment, acetylation with acetic anhydride, and silane coupling agent (e.g., 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane) treatment. The modified fibers will be characterized using FTIR spectroscopy and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) to confirm chemical changes. Composite samples will be fabricated via compression molding with polypropylene. Interfacial adhesion will be assessed through micromechanical testing (single fiber pull-out) and macro-mechanical testing (tensile and flexural properties). Moisture sorption behavior will be evaluated using dynamic vapor sorption (DVS) analysis to link chemical modification to hygroscopic performance.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a direct, comparative evaluation of established wood fiber treatments for flax fiber modification, establishing clear structure-property relationships between chemical surface changes, interfacial adhesion, and composite performance. It will deliver a practical framework for selecting and optimizing fiber treatments for flax-based biocomposites, contributing to their viability in durable applications such as building cladding components.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the systematic transfer and side-by-side evaluation of a suite of chemical methods from the mature field of wood composite science to flax fibers, explicitly connecting these treatments to the moisture sorption behavior and durability concerns central to Dr. Hill's expertise in wood modification and archaeological conservation.

proposals.title: Hygro-mechanical performance of nanocellulose-basalt microfiber composites: A model system for understanding modified wood durability
proposals.motivation: The long-term performance of modified wood and bio-based composites in construction is critically dependent on their moisture sorption behaviour and dimensional stability. Pre-2026 research on wood modification and archaeological wood conservation highlights the need for fundamental models linking nano/micro-scale reinforcement to macro-scale hygro-mechanical properties. Developing such models requires well-defined composite systems that mimic wood's hierarchical structure but with controlled variables.
proposals.hypothesis: That a composite of nanocellulose (from flax) and basalt microfibers in a bio-based polymer matrix will exhibit synergistic hygro-mechanical properties, where the nanocellulose reduces moisture sorption kinetics and the basalt fibres mitigate the resultant swelling stresses, leading to enhanced durability compared to the individual components.
proposals.method: Flax-derived cellulose nanocrystals (CNCs) and nanofibrils (CNFs) will be isolated using pre-2026 standard mechanical and acid hydrolysis methods. These will be combined with basalt microfibers in a polylactic acid (PLA) matrix using solvent casting and compression moulding to create a series of composite films and small test specimens. The hygroscopic behaviour will be characterized using dynamic vapour sorption (DVS) analysis, and the mechanical performance under controlled humidity cycles will be assessed via dynamic mechanical analysis (DMA). Microstructural analysis will use scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and X-ray micro-computed tomography (µCT).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a fundamental, material science-based understanding of how nano- and micro-scale reinforcements interact to control moisture-related degradation in bio-composites. It will deliver a novel, tunable model material system for testing durability hypotheses relevant to thermally modified wood and external cladding, bridging the gap between wood science and composite engineering.
proposals.novelty: While nanocellulose and basalt fibres have been studied separately, their combined use in a model composite designed explicitly to deconvolute moisture sorption and mechanical reinforcement mechanisms in a wood-analogue system is novel. This approach applies principles from archaeological wood conservation (understanding degradation) to the forward design of new, durable biocomposites.

proposals.title: Decoupling Non-Capillary Sorption Hysteresis in Modified Wood Composites for Predictive Service Life Modelling
proposals.motivation: The long-term durability of wood composites in construction is critically limited by moisture-induced damage. Current service-life models rely heavily on empirical correlations with capillary water uptake, failing to account for the underlying molecular-scale sorption hysteresis that governs moisture retention and mechanical degradation over decades. This gap is particularly acute for modified wood composites (e.g., thermally modified timber), where traditional pore structure is altered, making fundamental, physics-based predictions essential for material development and conservation.
proposals.hypothesis: The primary hysteresis in moisture sorption isotherms of modified wood composites is governed by non-capillary mechanisms—specifically, the irreversible relaxation of the cell wall polymer matrix and chemisorption at modified chemical sites—and this hysteresis directly controls the accumulation of hygro-mechanical damage during environmental aging.
proposals.method: The research will employ a sequential experimental decoupling approach. Dynamic Vapour Sorption (DVS) analysis with protocol-controlled adsorption-desorption cycles will be used on microtomed samples of thermally modified and acetylated wood to isolate hysteresis loops. These will be analysed using pre-2026 models like the Parallel Exponential Kinetics (PEK) model and the Hailwood-Horrobin model to separate contributions from different sorption sites. Complementary techniques will include nanoindentation to assess cell wall softening and FTIR spectroscopy to track irreversible chemical changes. The data will inform a modified Kelvin-Voigt viscoelastic model, integrating sorption kinetics with mechanical property decay.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first mechanistic framework linking specific non-capillary sorption phenomena to the long-term hygro-mechanical performance of modified wood. It will yield a predictive model for service life based on fundamental material physics, moving beyond empirical correlations. The findings will directly inform the development of next-generation wood composites with intrinsically superior moisture resistance for both new construction and the conservation of waterlogged archaeological wood.
proposals.novelty: While sorption hysteresis in wood is well-studied, this proposal uniquely targets its non-capillary origins in modified composites as the primary driver of long-term degradation, a shift from the prevailing focus on capillary transport. The explicit experimental decoupling of polymer relaxation and chemisorption effects from pore condensation is a novel approach to durability prediction.

proposals.title: A Continuum Mechanics Model for Moisture-Dependent Wood Properties: Quantifying the Diffuse Fibre Saturation Transition Zone
proposals.motivation: The fibre saturation point (FSP) is a fundamental concept in wood science, yet its treatment as a single threshold is a significant simplification that limits predictive accuracy in engineering and conservation. This is particularly critical for modelling dimensional stability, mechanical performance, and moisture transport in applications ranging from building cladding to archaeological wood conservation. A framework that captures the continuous, history-dependent transition of properties across the hygroscopic range is needed to replace this outdated assumption.
proposals.hypothesis: The FSP is not a discrete boundary but a diffuse transition zone, the breadth and property-specific midpoint of which can be quantitatively predicted by integrating sorption thermodynamics with the mechanics of a swelling polymer-gel composite. This apparent FSP will vary systematically with wood species, thermal modification history, and temperature.
proposals.method: The research will develop a multi-scale continuum model coupling a Guggenheim-Anderson-de Boer (GAB) sorption isotherm with a swelling stress formulation for the cell wall polymer matrix. Microstructural heterogeneity will be incorporated via a Representative Volume Element (RVE) approach, informed by micro-CT data. The model will be implemented in a finite element framework (e.g., COMSOL Multiphysics) to simulate the continuous evolution of elastic modulus, strain, and permeability with moisture content. Model parameters will be inversely identified from dynamic vapour sorption (DVS) and nanoindentation data on control and thermally modified wood samples.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a robust, physics-based engineering tool that supersedes the single-threshold FSP, enabling accurate prediction of wood behaviour in variable environments. It will generate novel, property-specific 'apparent FSP' metrics and quantify their dependence on material history, directly benefiting fields from timber engineering to the conservation of waterlogged archaeological wood.
proposals.novelty: While the limitations of a fixed FSP are acknowledged, no pre-2026 continuum mechanics framework explicitly models it as a diffuse zone to predict the continuous evolution of multiple key engineering properties. The integration of sorption hysteresis, polymer gel mechanics, and microstructural representation to define property-specific transition points is a novel synthesis.

proposals.title: A Multi-Scale Lattice Model for Cooperative Water Sorption in Wood: Integrating Atomistic Simulations and X-ray Tomography Validation
proposals.motivation: Classical sorption models for wood, based on independent site assumptions, fail to accurately predict hysteresis and swelling, which are critical for assessing dimensional stability, durability, and long-term performance in construction and conservation. This gap limits the predictive engineering of timber structures and the conservation of waterlogged archaeological wood. A model that captures cooperative sorption and material heterogeneity is urgently needed.
proposals.hypothesis: Water sorption in wood is governed by cooperative interactions between sorption sites and material heterogeneity; a multi-scale lattice model parameterized by atomistic simulations will provide superior predictions of sorption isotherms, hysteresis, and swelling strain compared to independent-site models.
proposals.method: The project will first use molecular dynamics (MD) simulations with the CHARMM force field to quantify water-cellulose/hemicellulose binding energies and nearest-neighbor interaction parameters. These parameters will inform a 3D Ising-type lattice model, solved via Monte Carlo simulation, incorporating site energy distributions from wood's anatomical heterogeneity. Model predictions for sorption and swelling will be validated against dynamic vapor sorption (DVS) data and 3D swelling fields obtained from moisture-conditioned X-ray computed tomography (XCT) on modified (e.g., thermally modified) and unmodified wood samples.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a validated, predictive framework for moisture sorption in wood that explicitly accounts for cooperative effects, directly linking molecular interactions to macroscopic behavior. It will provide a quantitative tool for assessing the moisture-related performance of modified wood in engineering applications and inform conservation strategies for archaeological wood by improving durability predictions.
proposals.novelty: The explicit integration of atomistically-derived cooperative interaction parameters into a heterogeneous lattice model for wood sorption is a novel approach, moving beyond mean-field and independent-site theories that dominate pre-2026 wood science.

proposals.title: Beyond Langmuir: Quantifying Cooperative and Competitive Water Sorption in Modified and Archaeological Wood Using Dynamic Vapour Sorption and NMR Relaxometry
proposals.motivation: The sorption behaviour of water in wood underpins dimensional stability, durability, and conservation strategies. Current models, often based on Langmuir-type assumptions of independent, identical sites, fail to capture the complex, heterogeneous reality of wood's cell wall matrix, especially after modification or degradation. A more accurate physical description is critical for advancing wood modification technologies and improving conservation protocols for waterlogged archaeological wood.
proposals.hypothesis: Water sorption sites in the wood cell wall are not independent and identical; instead, they exhibit cooperative (facilitated) and competitive (hindered) interactions, the balance of which is fundamentally altered by thermal modification and archaeological degradation processes.
proposals.method: Dynamic Vapour Sorption (DVS) will be used to generate detailed sorption isotherms for matched sets of native, thermally modified, and degraded archaeological wood samples. The data will be analysed using a Generalized D’Arcy and Watt model, which incorporates terms for primary and secondary sorption with site interaction parameters. This will be complemented by low-field NMR relaxometry (T1 and T2) to probe the population dynamics and mobility of water populations at different relative humidities, providing a direct experimental measure of site heterogeneity and interaction.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new, physically grounded framework for modelling moisture sorption in complex lignocellulosic materials. It will deliver quantitative parameters describing how modification and degradation alter the cooperative/competitive landscape of sorption, directly informing the life prediction of modified wood in service and the development of more nuanced drying schedules for conserved artefacts.
proposals.novelty: While sorption models are well-established, explicitly testing for and quantifying site interaction effects in wood using a combined modelling and NMR approach is novel. Applying this framework to contrast the effects of deliberate modification (thermal) with long-term degradation (archaeological) provides a unique comparative lens.

proposals.title: Modelling Water Plasticization in Wood as a Natural Polymer Blend: Applying the Gordon-Taylor Equation to Predict Glass Transition Depression
proposals.motivation: Understanding the molecular-level softening of wood by water is critical for predicting its mechanical behaviour during processing, its performance in service, and its long-term durability, especially in variable climates. Current models for wood's hygromechanical behaviour are largely empirical, lacking a rigorous thermodynamic foundation that links moisture content directly to the glass transition of its amorphous components (lignin, hemicellulose). Establishing such a link would provide a fundamental, predictive framework applicable to wood modification, composite design, and the conservation of waterlogged archaeological wood.
proposals.hypothesis: The depression of the glass transition temperature (Tg) in wood with increasing moisture content follows the thermodynamic principles of polymer plasticization and can be accurately modelled by adapting the Gordon-Taylor equation, treating wood as a binary blend of amorphous polymers and water as a plasticizing diluent.
proposals.method: The research will employ Dynamic Mechanical Analysis (DMA) and Differential Scanning Calorimetry (DSC) to measure the Tg of small, defect-free wood samples (e.g., earlywood of a softwood like Scots pine) equilibrated to precise moisture contents from 0% to the fibre saturation point. The experimental Tg-composition data will be fitted to the Gordon-Taylor equation, Tg = (w1Tg1 + k w2Tg2) / (w1 + k w2), where w1 and w2 are the weight fractions of wood polymer and water, Tg1 and Tg2 are their respective pure-component Tgs, and k is an interaction parameter quantifying plasticization efficiency. The model's fit and the derived k parameter will be evaluated for different wood species and compared to values for synthetic polymer-water systems.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative, thermodynamics-based model for water plasticization in wood, moving beyond descriptive correlations. It will yield a fundamental material parameter (the Gordon-Taylor k) that characterizes the strength of water-polymer interactions in wood, offering a new tool for predicting moisture-dependent viscoelasticity. This framework can directly inform the development of more durable wood-based materials and improved conservation protocols for wet archaeological wood.
proposals.novelty: While the Gordon-Taylor equation is standard in synthetic polymer science, its rigorous application to the complex, natural polymer blend of wood to quantify water plasticization is novel. This approach transcends traditional sorption isotherm analysis by directly linking moisture content to a key material transition temperature via an established thermodynamic model.

proposals.title: A Hygro-Mechanical Model for the Climate-Dependent Optimisation of Fibre Loading in Wood-Polymer Composites
proposals.motivation: Wood-polymer composites (WPCs) are widely used in building cladding and decking, where they are exposed to fluctuating humidity. Current design assumes a static optimal fibre loading, but moisture sorption plasticises both wood fibres and the polymer matrix, critically altering interfacial stress transfer. This gap leads to potential over-engineering in dry conditions or underperformance in wet service environments, impacting material efficiency and lifecycle performance.
proposals.hypothesis: The optimal fibre loading for mechanical performance in a WPC is not static but exists within a performance envelope defined by environmental moisture content, due to moisture-mediated shifts in the glass transition temperature (Tg) of the fibre-matrix interphase region and the resulting changes in composite toughness.
proposals.method: We will fabricate polypropylene-based WPCs with controlled fibre loadings (20-60 wt.%) using a twin-screw extruder. Samples will be conditioned to precise equilibrium moisture contents (EMCs) using dynamic vapour sorption (DVS). The Tg depression of the wood fibre surface and polymer will be quantified using Dynamic Mechanical Analysis (DMA) and modelled using the Gordon-Taylor equation. Interfacial shear strength will be measured via single fibre pull-out tests under controlled humidity. A finite element model, incorporating the moisture-dependent Tg and interfacial properties, will be developed to predict the composite's stress-strain behaviour and identify the fibre loading that maximises toughness for a given EMC.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a predictive, hygro-mechanical framework for tailoring WPC formulation to specific climatic conditions, moving beyond empirical rules. It will provide a fundamental understanding of how moisture plasticisation dictates the fibre-matrix interphase performance. The outcome is a practical methodology for optimising material use and economic cost over a product's service life.
proposals.novelty: While moisture effects on wood and polymers are separately well-studied, this research uniquely integrates sorption behaviour, interfacial mechanics, and composite modelling to dynamically redefine 'optimal' fibre loading based on environmental humidity, challenging the static high-loading paradigm.

proposals.title: Decoupling Component Dynamics in the Wood Cell Wall: A Percolation-Based Framework for Hydrated Polymer Composites
proposals.motivation: The physics of hydrated polymer composites, critical for biomaterials and advanced biocomposites, is often oversimplified using models from synthetic polymers, which fail to capture the hierarchical complexity of natural systems like wood. A fundamental gap exists in understanding how a percolating nanofiber network (cellulose) mechanically constrains the glass transition and moisture sorption behavior of the surrounding plasticized amorphous matrix (lignin/hemicellulose). This limits the predictive design of both wood modification processes and bio-inspired synthetic materials.
proposals.hypothesis: The mechanical percolation of the cellulose microfibril network imposes a spatial and temporal constraint on the glass transition of the lignin-hemicellulose matrix, decoupling its apparent softening from the intrinsic transitions of its individual components, and this effect can be quantified via moisture-dependent dynamic mechanical analysis.
proposals.method: We will use a suite of targeted experiments on wood samples (native, thermally modified, and delignified) to probe component-specific transitions: Dynamic Mechanical Analysis (DMA) with moisture conditioning, Dielectric Spectroscopy (DES), and 1H NMR relaxometry. These data will parameterize a coarse-grained computational model using the LAMMPS molecular dynamics engine, implementing a fiber network (cellulose) with tunable connectivity within a viscoelastic matrix (lignin/hemicellulose) whose properties vary with simulated moisture content. The model will explicitly test percolation thresholds and network effects on composite Tg.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a first-principles, multi-scale physical framework that defines the role of nanofiber percolation in composite hygro-mechanical behavior. It will directly advance the fundamental understanding of wood moisture sorption and thermal modification. The validated model will provide a new design tool for engineering synthetic biocomposites with tailored moisture responsiveness and mechanical performance.
proposals.novelty: Moving beyond treating wood as a homogenous polymer blend, this project explicitly decouples and quantifies the constrained dynamics of the amorphous matrix, a non-obvious approach given the historical focus on bulk wood properties. The integration of targeted experimental probes with a percolation-based computational model represents a novel synthesis of wood science and soft matter physics.

proposals.title: A Multi-Scale Modelling and Design of Experiments Framework for the Targeted Hygro-Mechanical Optimisation of Modified Wood
proposals.motivation: The dimensional instability of wood due to moisture sorption remains a primary limitation for its use in high-performance applications, driving the need for chemical or thermal modifications. Current development of these treatments is largely empirical and resource-intensive, lacking a systematic link between the fundamental mechanisms of modification at the molecular/cell-wall scale and the resulting macroscopic hygro-mechanical performance. A targeted, data-efficient approach is urgently needed to accelerate the design of dimensionally stable wood products for the built environment.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesise that a multi-scale physical model can identify a minimal set of key molecular-scale parameters (e.g., hydroxyl accessibility, lignin cross-link density) that govern bulk hygro-expansion, and that a statistical Design of Experiments (DoE) framework can then efficiently map the complex, non-linear effects of modification treatments on these parameters to predict optimal product stability.
proposals.method: We will develop a hierarchical model linking atomistic simulations (e.g., molecular dynamics of water-cellulose/lignin interactions) to a micro-mechanical model of the wood cell wall to predict hygro-expansion coefficients. Sensitivity analysis will identify the 2-3 most critical input parameters. A D-optimal DoE will then be employed to design a minimal experimental matrix of thermal and chemical modifications (e.g., furfurylation, acetylation, thermal treatment) that systematically vary these key parameters. The macroscopic moisture sorption behaviour and dimensional change of the treated samples will be characterised, and the data used to construct a predictive response surface model linking treatment variables to performance.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, integrated pipeline that fundamentally connects wood modification chemistry to engineering performance, moving beyond trial-and-error. It will provide a validated, data-efficient methodology for optimising treatment recipes for dimensional stability and yield specific treatment protocols that minimise hygro-expansion in engineered wood products.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the explicit fusion of a bottom-up, physics-based multi-scale model with a top-down statistical DoE framework to guide experimental design, creating a closed-loop, mechanism-targeted optimisation strategy for wood modification that is currently absent from the literature.

proposals.title: A Multi-Scale Homogenization Framework for Predicting Hygro-Mechanical Behaviour in Engineered Wood Products
proposals.motivation: The long-term performance of mass timber structures like glulam and CLT is critically dependent on their response to fluctuating humidity, which induces internal stresses, warping, and creep. Current design models often rely on empirical adjustments, lacking a physics-based foundation to predict moisture-related degradation over decades. A robust, predictive model is essential for improving durability assessments and enabling the safe, wider use of engineered wood in construction.
proposals.hypothesis: The macroscopic hygro-mechanical behaviour of engineered wood can be accurately predicted by homogenizing fundamental, anisotropic sorption and swelling mechanisms at the wood lamina level, incorporating the constraining effects of adhesive layers, using techniques adapted from composite micromechanics.
proposals.method: The research will develop a computational multi-scale framework. At the micro-scale, a Representative Volume Element (RVE) will model wood lamellae (with anisotropic moisture diffusivity and hygro-expansion) bonded by an adhesive layer. Micromechanical models (e.g., Mori-Tanaka, Self-Consistent schemes) will homogenize the RVE's hygro-elastic properties. This will be implemented within a Finite Element Analysis (FEA) environment, using user-defined material subroutines (UMATs) to upscale local moisture-state-dependent properties to predict macroscopic warping and stress distributions in component-scale models under transient hygrothermal conditions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, physics-based computational tool for engineers to simulate moisture-induced stresses and deformations in timber composites, moving beyond empirical rules. It will contribute fundamental understanding of how adhesive constraints and wood variability influence long-term performance, directly informing improved design standards for durable mass timber construction.
proposals.novelty: While homogenization is standard for synthetic composites, its rigorous application to model moisture-driven behaviour in the hierarchical, bio-based composite of engineered wood is novel. This approach integrates wood science sorption models with composite mechanics upscaling techniques to create a predictive framework for a previously intractable multi-physics problem.

proposals.title: Linking Hot-Press Process Parameters to Moisture Sorption and Internal Stress Development in Engineered Wood Bond Lines
proposals.motivation: The long-term dimensional stability and durability of mass timber elements like glulam and CLT are critically dependent on moisture-induced stresses, which can originate at the wood-adhesive bond line. Current manufacturing optimization focuses on short-term mechanical properties, leaving a knowledge gap in how hot-press parameters (temperature, pressure, time) influence the fundamental hygroscopic behaviour and internal stress state that governs long-term performance under environmental cycling. This disconnect poses a risk to the engineered reliability of timber structures, especially as their use in external applications expands.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific combinations of hot-press parameters induce quantifiable, permanent changes to the wood's sorption properties and the internal stress state of the bond line, and that these changes are the primary predictors of long-term hygro-mechanical performance in engineered wood products.
proposals.method: We will systematically manufacture wood-adhesive samples across a designed matrix of temperature, pressure, and pressing time. Sorption isotherms will be measured using dynamic vapour sorption (DSA) to quantify changes in hygroscopicity. Internal stresses will be characterized using digital image correlation (DIC) on restrained specimens during moisture cycling. The resulting dataset will parameterize a multi-scale finite element model, built using the commercial software ABAQUS with user-defined material subroutines (UMATs), that simulates moisture diffusion and mechanical stress development from the bond line to the full-scale element.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a validated, predictive modelling framework that directly links factory settings to long-term hygro-mechanical risk. It will provide manufacturers with a science-based protocol for selecting hot-press parameters that minimize moisture-related performance degradation. The research will advance the fundamental understanding of how thermo-hydro-mechanical processing alters wood's hygroscopic response.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in fusing process parameter studies—common in thermal modification research—with detailed sorption analysis and internal stress quantification to create a predictive model for engineered wood, an approach not yet established in pre-2026 literature. It moves beyond correlating parameters with strength to explicitly modelling the driver of long-term failure: moisture-induced stress.

proposals.title: Quantifying Pore System Alterations in Anhydride-Modified Timber via Mercury Intrusion Porosimetry: Linking Microstructure to Hygroscopic Performance
proposals.motivation: Chemical modification, particularly with anhydrides, is a key method for enhancing wood's dimensional stability and durability for external cladding and conservation. However, the fundamental microstructural changes—specifically pore size distribution (PSD)—that underpin the well-documented reductions in hygroscopicity and improvements in long-term performance are inferred rather than directly measured. A direct, quantitative analysis of these alterations is critical for optimizing modification processes and predicting material behaviour in service.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that anhydride modification of wood (e.g., with acetic or succinic anhydride) systematically alters the pore size distribution, specifically reducing the volume of micropores and small mesopores associated with water sorption in the cell wall, and that these measured changes will correlate directly with observed reductions in equilibrium moisture content and hysteresis.
proposals.method: We will apply Mercury Intrusion Porosimetry (MIP) to directly quantify the pore size distribution in control and modified wood samples (e.g., Scots pine sapwood). Samples will be prepared to a constant moisture content and subjected to a standard anhydride modification process. MIP analysis will be conducted using a standard porosimeter (e.g., Micromeritics AutoPore series), with pressure increments carefully selected to cover the pore size range from approximately 0.005 to 200 µm. Data will be processed using the Washburn equation to convert intrusion pressure to pore diameter, generating cumulative and differential intrusion curves. Complementary dynamic vapour sorption (DVS) measurements will be performed to correlate PSD changes with moisture sorption isotherms.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct, quantitative dataset on pore system evolution in anhydride-modified wood, moving beyond inferred mechanisms. It will establish a clear microstructure-property link, offering a materials science foundation for the observed hygroscopic improvements. The findings will inform more targeted modification strategies for both contemporary timber engineering and the conservation of waterlogged archaeological wood, where understanding cell wall accessibility is paramount.
proposals.novelty: While MIP is established in materials science, its systematic application to chemically modified wood to directly test long-standing hypotheses about cell wall bulking and pore filling is novel. This approach bridges a gap between traditional wood science characterization and rigorous porous materials analysis, offering a new lens on modification efficacy.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Solvent-Accessible Network: A Polymer Swelling Analysis of Thermally Modified and Acetylated Timber for Enhanced Dimensional Stability Prediction
proposals.motivation: The dimensional instability of wood in response to moisture remains a critical limitation for its use in construction and conservation. While thermal and chemical modifications are known to improve stability by altering the wood cell wall polymer matrix, current sorption isotherm methods primarily assess water interactions, providing an incomplete picture of the underlying structural changes. A polymer science approach, using a range of solvents with different polarities and molecular sizes, can directly probe the accessibility and cross-linking of the lignocellulosic network, offering deeper mechanistic insight than water sorption alone.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the reduction in equilibrium swelling of modified wood in a suite of solvents will correlate directly with the severity of the modification treatment and that the pattern of swelling across solvents will reveal whether stability is achieved primarily through hemicellulose degradation (thermal modification) or cell wall bulking and esterification (acetylation).
proposals.method: We will prepare matched samples of Scots pine sapwood, including untreated controls, thermally modified (at 180°C, 200°C, 220°C), and acetylated specimens. Swelling will be measured gravimetrically and dimensionally in a series of solvents (water, methanol, dimethyl sulfoxide, n-hexane) following established polymer network protocols. The Flory-Rehner theory will be applied to calculate the average molecular weight between cross-links (Mc) from the equilibrium swelling data in the best solvent, providing a quantitative measure of the network density. Complementary FTIR spectroscopy and dynamic vapour sorption (DVS) will link swelling parameters to chemical changes and traditional hygroscopicity.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel, quantitative framework for comparing the efficacy of different wood modification systems based on fundamental polymer network parameters. It will provide a more predictive model for long-term dimensional behaviour in variable environments, bridging the gap between wood science and polymer physics. The findings will be directly applicable to optimizing modification processes for both new construction materials and conservation treatments for archaeological wood.
proposals.novelty: While swelling in water is commonly measured, the systematic application of polymer network theory and a multi-solvent approach to characterize commercially relevant modified wood is novel. This moves beyond descriptive hygroscopicity to a fundamental, quantitative analysis of the cell wall as a cross-linked polymer gel.

proposals.title: Decay Resistance at the Microscale: A Single-Fiber Analysis of Modified Wood
proposals.motivation: Current understanding of wood decay resistance after modification (e.g., thermal, chemical) is based on bulk or small-block tests, which average effects across heterogeneous tissues. This masks critical variability in decay susceptibility between cell types (e.g., earlywood vs. latewood tracheids, fibers) and fails to identify the precise anatomical and chemical loci of protection. A microscale approach is essential to link specific modification-induced changes in cell wall chemistry and moisture sorption behavior to localized decay resistance, offering a more fundamental mechanism for durability.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the decay resistance imparted by wood modification treatments is not uniform at the cellular level, and that latewood tracheids or specific fiber types will exhibit significantly greater resistance due to their inherent anatomical and chemical differences, which are differentially altered by modification processes.
proposals.method: Individual wood fibers and tracheids will be isolated from modified (thermally modified and acetylated) and unmodified control wood samples using maceration techniques. These isolated cells will be subjected to controlled decay environments using standardized brown-rot (e.g., *Gloeophyllum trabeum*) and white-rot fungi in micro-culture assays. Decay progression will be quantified using pre-2026 micro-analytical techniques: (1) micro-spectroscopy (FTIR-ATR) to track chemical changes (e.g., lignin/carbohydrate ratios, acetyl group presence) in individual fibers, and (2) dynamic vapor sorption (DVS) analysis on fiber bundles to correlate hygroscopic behavior with decay susceptibility. Imaging will be performed using scanning electron microscopy (SEM) to visualize hyphal penetration and cell wall degradation.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel, high-resolution methodology for assessing wood durability, moving beyond bulk properties. It will provide the first direct evidence of how modification treatments protect specific wood cells, linking micro-scale moisture sorption behavior to decay resistance. The findings will inform more targeted and efficient modification processes and contribute fundamental knowledge to the conservation of archaeological waterlogged wood, where cell-level degradation is critical.
proposals.novelty: While bulk decay tests are standard, systematically testing the decay resistance of isolated, individual wood fibers or tracheids from modified wood is a novel, reductionist approach. It uniquely bridges the gap between macroscopic durability performance and the microscale chemical and hygroscopic properties of specific cell types, a scale not addressed in prior review literature on thermal modification or conservation.

proposals.title: Pilot-scale acetylation of structural timber: dimensional stability and decay resistance of large cross-sections
proposals.motivation: Acetylation is a leading commercial wood modification technology proven to enhance durability and dimensional stability in cladding and joinery. However, its application to large-dimension structural timber (e.g., glulam beams, CLT panels) remains underexplored at an industrial scale. Scaling up presents significant challenges, as achieving uniform reagent penetration and reaction in thick sections is non-trivial, and the long-term performance of such large-scale modified elements is unknown, limiting its use in engineered wood construction.
proposals.hypothesis: That industrial-scale acetylation can be successfully applied to large structural timber sections (≥ 100mm thickness) to achieve a uniform and sufficient degree of modification, resulting in significantly improved resistance to moisture sorption and fungal decay compared to untreated wood of the same dimensions.
proposals.method: Large-dimension specimens of a commercially relevant softwood (e.g., Scots pine or Radiata pine) will be processed in an industrial acetylation plant (e.g., Accoya® production facility). The treatment process parameters (vacuum/pressure cycles, temperature, time) will be monitored and adjusted for thick sections. Post-treatment, cores will be extracted radially and tangentially to assess the uniformity of acetyl content via near-infrared spectroscopy or titration. Treated and matched untreated controls will undergo extensive hygroscopic testing (dynamic vapour sorption, water immersion) and biological testing (basidiomycete fungi according to EN 113) to evaluate decay performance.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive dataset on the efficacy of industrial acetylation for structural-sized timber, directly informing standards and building codes. It will bridge the gap between laboratory-scale chemical modification research and the practical requirements of the mass timber construction industry, potentially unlocking a new class of highly durable, bio-based structural materials.
proposals.novelty: While acetylation is well-studied for small samples, this research explicitly targets the scale-up gap by focusing on the industrial process and performance of large cross-sections, integrating pre-2026 expertise in wood modification, moisture sorption behaviour, and archaeological wood conservation principles related to cell wall bulking and decay arrest.

proposals.title: Beyond Hydroxyls: Identifying the Dominant Chemical and Structural Drivers of Hygroscopicity in Thermally Modified Timber
proposals.motivation: The hygroscopic reduction in thermally modified wood (TMW) is almost universally attributed to the loss of accessible hydroxyl groups. However, this established model fails to fully explain observed sorption hysteresis, variability between wood species, and long-term moisture behaviour in service. A more nuanced understanding is critical for optimising TMW for durable external cladding and for informing conservation strategies for both modified and archaeological wood, where moisture interactions dictate lifespan.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesise that the hygroscopicity of TMW is governed by a complex interplay of factors beyond hydroxyl accessibility, including the formation of cross-linking structures, changes in amorphous polymer matrix properties, and the role of re-deposited hydrophobic compounds from hemicellulose degradation.
proposals.method: We will employ a multi-analytical approach on matched samples of spruce and ash before and after thermal modification (ThermoWood process). Dynamic vapour sorption (DVS) analysis will provide precise isotherms and hysteresis data. Chemical changes will be quantified using FTIR spectroscopy (focusing on carbonyl and ether bands) and wet chemistry for hydroxyl content. Nano-structural analysis will be conducted using nitrogen porosimetry and small-angle X-ray scattering (SAXS). Statistical modelling (principal component analysis and multiple linear regression) will correlate chemical/structural parameters with equilibrium moisture content to identify dominant drivers.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a refined, evidence-based model for moisture sorption in TMW, moving beyond the simplistic hydroxyl-centric view. It will deliver predictive relationships between specific chemical modifications and hygroscopic performance, aiding in the targeted development of more stable timber products for construction and offering new insights for the conservation of degraded wooden artefacts.
proposals.novelty: This directly challenges a foundational assumption in wood science by systematically decoupling hydroxyl loss from other concurrent chemical changes. It integrates sorption analysis with nano-structural probes rarely combined in TMW studies, seeking to identify the primary, rather than assumed, hygroscopic drivers.

proposals.title: Catalytic Hydrothermal Modification of Wood: A Synergistic Approach to Control Degradation Pathways and Enhance Hygroscopic Stability
proposals.motivation: Conventional thermal modification (TM) of wood is typically performed under dry or inert gas conditions to limit oxidative degradation, which restricts process flexibility and the range of achievable properties. Recent reviews, including Hill's own work, highlight that hydrothermal treatments can induce different chemical pathways but often lead to excessive hydrolysis and strength loss. There is a significant gap in understanding how the deliberate introduction of chemical catalysts during wet thermal treatment could steer these degradation reactions towards more desirable outcomes, improving efficiency and property control.
proposals.hypothesis: The simultaneous application of specific organic acid catalysts (e.g., citric, oxalic) or metal ions during hydrothermal treatment will selectively promote hemicellulose depolymerisation and subsequent re-condensation reactions over cellulose degradation, leading to a wood material with superior and more predictable hygroscopic stability and biological durability compared to standard thermal modification.
proposals.method: Small, clear wood samples (e.g., Scots pine sapwood) will be impregnated with aqueous solutions of selected catalysts at varying concentrations. A full-factorial experimental design will be used, with treatment temperature (160-180°C), time (2-6 hours), and catalyst type/concentration as independent variables. The process will be conducted in sealed reactors under saturated steam conditions. The primary response variables will be mass loss, equilibrium moisture content (EMC) determined by dynamic vapour sorption (DVS), and chemical composition analysed by FTIR and wet chemistry. The degradation pathways will be inferred by correlating the chemical changes with the resultant hygroscopic and mechanical properties.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel process protocol that merges catalytic chemistry with hydrothermal wood modification. It will provide a mechanistic understanding of how catalysts alter the reaction kinetics and pathways during thermal treatment. The findings will contribute directly to the development of more efficient and tunable wood modification technologies, with potential applications in producing dimensionally stable cladding and in the conservation of waterlogged wood by informing on degradation mechanisms.
proposals.novelty: While hydrothermal treatment and catalytic wood modification have been studied separately, their intentional combination to *steer* thermal degradation for property enhancement is novel. This approach fundamentally relaxes the 'isolated thermal treatment' condition by introducing a controlled chemical environment, moving beyond mere process variation into a new paradigm of chemically-augmented thermohydromechanical processing.

proposals.title: Comparative Analysis of Biological Durability in Thermally Modified Timber: The Influence of Wet vs. Dry Process Conditions
proposals.motivation: Thermal modification (TMT) is a key commercial technology for enhancing wood durability without biocides. Pre-2026 literature, including reviews by Hill, extensively documents chemical changes and hygroscopicity but reveals a significant gap: a systematic, controlled comparison of the biological durability imparted by the two primary industrial process variants—wet (e.g., steam-saturated) and dry (e.g., inert gas or oil) conditions. Understanding if and how the process medium influences long-term performance is critical for optimizing industrial protocols and material specification for external applications like cladding.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the process medium (wet vs. dry) during thermal modification leads to quantifiable differences in the chemical substrate of wood, resulting in statistically significant variations in resistance to decay fungi and moisture sorption behaviour, which are the primary determinants of in-service durability.
proposals.method: Identical batches of a commercially relevant softwood (e.g., Scots pine sapwood) and hardwood (e.g., ash) will be thermally modified to equivalent mass loss levels (~8-12%) using a laboratory-scale reactor under rigorously controlled wet (superheated steam) and dry (nitrogen atmosphere) conditions. Durability will be assessed via standard EN 113/EN 350 bioassays against brown-rot (*Coniophora puteana*) and white-rot (*Trametes versicolor*) fungi. Complementary analysis will include: dynamic vapour sorption (DVS) to characterise hygroscopicity; FTIR spectroscopy to quantify key chemical markers (e.g., hydroxyl loss, carbonyl formation); and SEM to examine cell wall morphology. Statistical analysis (ANOVA) will correlate process parameters with durability metrics.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first controlled, mechanistic evidence directly linking TMT process type to biological durability outcomes. It will deliver a robust dataset to inform industrial process selection and material standards. Furthermore, by integrating sorption analysis—a core method in Hill's recent work on archaeological wood—with durability testing, it will advance the fundamental understanding of structure-property relationships in modified wood.
proposals.novelty: While the effects of temperature and duration on TMT properties are well-studied, a direct, systematic experimental comparison of wet and dry processes on durability is absent from pre-2026 literature. The novelty lies in isolating the 'process medium' variable while controlling for other factors and linking its effects to both biological performance and the underlying physicochemical changes.

proposals.title: Chemical Transformation of Extractives During Wet Thermal Modification of Wood: Pathways and Influence on Hygroscopicity and Durability
proposals.motivation: Wet thermal modification (e.g., hydrothermolysis) is an established industrial process for enhancing dimensional stability and decay resistance of wood, but its chemical mechanisms are less understood than dry thermal modification. Pre-2026 research has focused on hemicellulose and lignin degradation, largely overlooking the role of native extractives. Understanding the chemical fate of extractives is crucial, as their transformation products could significantly contribute to the modified wood's final properties, including its moisture sorption behavior and biological durability.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that during wet thermal modification, native wood extractives undergo specific chemical transformations (e.g., hydrolysis, condensation, Maillard-type reactions) that generate new, less-leachable compounds. These transformation products contribute directly to the observed reduction in equilibrium moisture content and enhanced resistance to fungal decay in the final material.
proposals.method: Wood samples (e.g., Scots pine sapwood) will undergo controlled wet thermal modification at varying temperatures (140-180°C) and durations. Sequential solvent extraction (dichloromethane, ethanol, water) will isolate native and transformed compounds from unmodified and modified wood. Extracts will be analyzed using GC-MS and Py-GC/MS for low-molecular-weight compounds and HPLC for phenolic constituents. The chemical data will be correlated with material properties measured via dynamic vapour sorption (DVS) to assess hygroscopicity and standard fungal tests (e.g., EN 113) for durability.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first detailed chemical mapping of extractive transformation pathways under wet thermal conditions, moving beyond the traditional focus on structural polymers. It will establish direct chemical-property relationships, offering a more complete mechanistic model for the process. The findings could inform process optimization to target specific property enhancements in modified wood products.
proposals.novelty: While the thermal degradation of wood polymers is well-reviewed, the deliberate study of extractive fate and its functional contribution in *wet* thermal modification is novel. This bridges the knowledge gap between wood modification chemistry and the established role of extractives in archaeological wood conservation, where similar aqueous thermal degradation processes occur over long timescales.

proposals.title: Chemometric Modelling of Thermal Degradation Pathways in Modified Timber: A Py-GC/MS and Multivariate Analysis Approach
proposals.motivation: Thermal modification of wood is a key industrial process for enhancing dimensional stability and durability, but its chemical pathways are complex and not fully predictive of final material properties. Current research often relies on correlating single chemical markers with performance, which oversimplifies the multivariate degradation process. This gap limits the precise control and optimization of modification protocols for different wood species and target applications.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that multivariate chemometric models, adapted from food pyrolysis studies, can identify specific patterns (fingerprints) of volatile organic compounds (VOCs) released during thermal modification that are robustly correlated with key final wood properties, such as hygroscopicity and durability.
proposals.method: Wood samples (softwood and hardwood) will undergo controlled thermal modification across a gradient of temperatures and durations. Pyrolysis-Gas Chromatography/Mass Spectrometry (Py-GC/MS) will generate complex VOC profiles for each treatment. These high-dimensional datasets will be analyzed using chemometric techniques standard in food science, specifically Principal Component Analysis (PCA) for exploratory data analysis and Partial Least Squares Regression (PLSR) to build predictive models linking VOC fingerprints to measured properties (equilibrium moisture content, mass loss, colour change).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, data-driven framework for understanding wood thermal degradation, moving beyond single-marker analysis. It will deliver predictive models that can help tailor modification processes for specific performance outcomes. The methodology could also offer new tools for diagnosing the state of archaeologically preserved waterlogged wood by analyzing its residual thermal degradation signature.
proposals.novelty: The direct application of chemometric models from food pyrolysis to wood science is novel, treating wood thermal degradation as a complex, multivariate chemical system rather than a series of isolated reactions. This cross-disciplinary approach has not been systematically explored in wood modification literature pre-2026.

proposals.title: A Quantitative Model for Predicting Moisture Sorption Isotherms in Modified and Archaeological Wood Based on Accessible Hydroxyl Group Concentration
proposals.motivation: The hygroscopic behaviour of wood, central to its dimensional stability and durability, is governed by the sorption of water onto hydroxyl groups in the cell wall polymers. Current predictive models, such as the Hailwood-Horrobin model, are phenomenological and do not explicitly link sorption to the fundamental, modifiable chemical substrate. A model based on accessible hydroxyl availability would provide a mechanistic framework for predicting the moisture performance of modified (e.g., thermally treated) and degraded (e.g., waterlogged archaeological) woods, which have altered hydroxyl populations.
proposals.hypothesis: The equilibrium moisture content (EMC) of wood at a given relative humidity can be predicted by a function of the concentration of accessible hydroxyl groups, which can be quantified using spectroscopic and chemical methods. This relationship holds across wood species and states of modification or degradation.
proposals.method: We will use a multi-scale experimental and modelling approach. First, a suite of wood samples (untreated, thermally modified, and chemically degraded to simulate archaeological states) will be characterised for accessible hydroxyl content using FTIR spectroscopy with deuterium exchange and chemical acetylation. Standard dynamic vapour sorption (DVS) will generate precise sorption isotherms. A multivariate regression model, incorporating hydroxyl accessibility data as the primary predictor, will be developed to predict the sorption isotherm parameters. The model will be validated against independent datasets from the literature on modified and archaeological wood.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative, chemistry-based model for predicting wood moisture sorption, moving beyond empirical fitting. It will offer a powerful tool for material scientists to design wood modifications for targeted hygroscopic performance and for conservators to better understand the moisture behaviour of degraded cultural heritage objects, informing conservation strategies.
proposals.novelty: While the role of hydroxyls in sorption is well-known, directly quantifying accessible hydroxyls and using this as the sole predictive variable for constructing full sorption isotherms across diverse wood states is a novel, mechanistic approach that departs from traditional curve-fitting models.

proposals.title: Hygrothermal Performance and Long-Term Service Life Modelling of Alternative Lignocellulosic Insulation Materials
proposals.motivation: The drive for sustainable construction has increased the use of bio-based insulation materials like straw bale and wood wool (excelsior). However, pre-2026 knowledge gaps exist regarding their long-term moisture sorption behaviour and durability under fluctuating environmental conditions, which are critical for reliable service life prediction and conservation of historic structures using such materials.
