A systematic, comparative analysis with modified wood is needed to inform material selection and preservation strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: Alternative lignocellulosic insulation materials (straw, wood wool) exhibit fundamentally different moisture sorption hysteresis and kinetic behaviour compared to thermally modified wood cladding, leading to distinct service life profiles under identical hygrothermal loads. Their degradation pathways are governed by their unique cell wall chemistry and microstructure.
proposals.method: The study will apply a consistent experimental framework used for timber to straw and wood wool. Dynamic vapour sorption (DVS) analysis will quantify sorption isotherms and hysteresis. Microscale specimens will be subjected to controlled cyclic humidity regimes within climate chambers, with mass and dimensional changes monitored. Chemical changes will be tracked via FTIR spectroscopy. Data will be integrated into existing service life prediction models (e.g., Factor Method) developed for wood, adapting degradation functions based on the observed moisture behaviour and known biochemical composition.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct, mechanistic comparison of the hygrothermal performance of major alternative bio-insulations against modified wood, generating essential property data for building physicists and material scientists. It will extend the applicability of wood-centric conservation and durability models to a broader class of lignocellulosic materials, supporting more accurate service life planning for sustainable and heritage buildings.
proposals.novelty: While moisture studies exist for individual materials, a unified experimental and modelling approach directly comparing the sorption kinetics and life prediction of straw, wood wool, and thermally modified wood is novel. Applying methodologies from archaeological wood conservation (understanding degradation in humid environments) to the performance assessment of modern bio-insulations represents a unique interdisciplinary synthesis.

proposals.title: Coupled Heat and Moisture Transfer in Thermally Modified Wood-Based Insulation: A Dynamic Sorption and Hygrothermal Modelling Study
proposals.motivation: The drive for sustainable construction has increased the use of bio-based insulation materials like wood fibre. However, their hygroscopic nature means moisture transport is intrinsically coupled to heat flow, critically influencing in-service durability, energy performance, and long-term stability, issues central to Hill's work on wood modification and archaeological conservation. A predictive understanding of these coupled phenomena under realistic, dynamic climatic conditions is lacking but essential for reliable material specification and building physics modelling.
proposals.hypothesis: Thermal modification of wood, by altering its chemical structure and reducing hygroscopicity, will decouple the moisture and heat transfer processes in wood-fibre insulation, leading to a more predictable and dampened hygrothermal response under dynamic boundary conditions compared to unmodified wood fibre.
proposals.method: The study will fabricate insulation mats from both unmodified and thermally modified wood fibres. Dynamic sorption experiments (DVS) will provide precise isotherms and kinetics data. These material properties will parameterise a numerical hygrothermal model (e.g., using Delphin or COMSOL with the Kunzel equation) to simulate coupled transfer. The model will be validated against experimental data from a custom-built climatic chamber subjecting samples to dynamic temperature and relative humidity cycles representative of external cladding conditions.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver validated, material-specific hygrothermal properties for modified wood insulation and a calibrated numerical model for predicting its in-service performance. It will provide a fundamental scientific link between thermal modification chemistry, sorption behaviour, and macroscopic heat/moisture transport, directly informing material development and building envelope design for improved durability and energy efficiency.
proposals.novelty: While heat and moisture transfer in building materials is studied, applying the lens of wood modification chemistry—specifically thermal modification—to decouple these processes in a bio-based insulation matrix under dynamic, realistic conditions is a novel synthesis of Hill's expertise in modification, sorption, and material life cycle.

proposals.title: Hygrothermal Performance of Full-Scale Wall Panels Incorporating Modified Wood and Bio-Based Insulation Boards in Simulated Service Climates
proposals.motivation: The drive for sustainable construction demands bio-based, carbon-storing materials like modified wood and wood-derived insulation. However, their long-term durability and moisture management in building envelopes remain a critical knowledge gap, risking performance failures. This research directly addresses this by moving beyond material-level studies to assess integrated system performance under realistic, dynamic conditions, which is essential for industry adoption and building code compliance.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that wall assemblies incorporating thermally modified wood cladding and wood-based insulation boards will demonstrate superior hygrothermal stability and reduced moisture accumulation risk compared to assemblies with unmodified wood, when subjected to dynamic temperature and humidity cycles representative of temperate and cold climates.
proposals.method: Full-scale wall panels will be constructed, featuring a control (untreated timber) and test (thermally modified timber) cladding, paired with wood fibre insulation boards. These will be subjected to dynamic hygrothermal cycles in a climate chamber, simulating annual weather profiles for a UK coastal and a Scandinavian climate. Performance will be monitored using embedded sensors for temperature and relative humidity at critical points (e.g., sheathing interface, within insulation), with data analysed to calculate moisture content, vapour pressure gradients, and mould growth risk indices using the VTT mould model.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comparative dataset on the whole-system hygrothermal performance of modified wood bio-based wall assemblies at full scale. It will deliver practical guidance on moisture-safe design details for architects and engineers, and generate critical performance data to support environmental product declarations and building physics simulations for these sustainable materials.
proposals.novelty: While material properties of modified wood are well-studied, its performance in a complete, vapour-permeable wall system with bio-based insulation under dynamic, realistic climatic loads is not established. This bridges the gap between small-sample sorption studies and real-world building physics, a necessary step for commercialization.

proposals.title: A comparative life cycle assessment of in-situ versus ex-situ synthesis of glucose-derived wood modification agents
proposals.motivation: The development of bio-based chemicals for wood modification, such as functionalized glucose substrates, is a key pre-2026 strategy for sustainable material science. However, the assumption that bio-based equates to low-impact is untested; the environmental burden of synthesizing these agents, especially via complex ex-situ chemical processes, could negate their benefits. A rigorous Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) is needed to guide the field towards truly sustainable modification pathways, aligning with Dr. Hill's expertise in wood modification and conservation lifecycles.
proposals.hypothesis: The in-situ functionalization of glucose during wood treatment processes will demonstrate a significantly lower overall environmental impact across its lifecycle than the conventional ex-situ synthesis, purification, and application of pre-formed glucose derivatives.
proposals.method: This study will employ a cradle-to-gate LCA (ISO 14040/44) comparing two routes for a model glucose-based hydrophobing agent. The ex-situ route will inventory all steps from glucose production, chemical functionalization in a reactor, purification, solvent recovery, and transport to a treatment facility. The in-situ route will model a one-pot process where glucose and reagents are introduced directly into the wood matrix, with reaction catalyzed by heat or microwave energy. Primary data will be sourced from lab-scale synthesis trials and literature, with impact assessment using the ReCiPe 2016 midpoint method, focusing on global warming potential, fossil resource scarcity, and freshwater ecotoxicity.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative environmental profile for emerging glucose-based wood modifiers, challenging assumptions about their green credentials. It will offer a critical decision-making framework for chemists and material scientists, prioritizing synthesis pathways that minimize overall impact. The findings will directly inform sustainable practice in timber modification and conservation, bridging Dr. Hill's research on chemical changes in wood and the lifecycle of archaeological materials.
proposals.novelty: While LCA is established for bulk chemicals and timber products, its application to novel, in-situ bio-based modification chemistries is unexplored. The study uniquely challenges the 'bio-based is best' paradigm within wood science by rigorously comparing synthesis location, a factor often overlooked in favour of evaluating only the final treated product's performance.

proposals.title: A Multi-Scale Physical-Mechanical Strategy for Bio-Compatibilization of Coir Fibres in High-Performance, Low-Chemical Composites
proposals.motivation: The demand for sustainable, bio-based composites is growing, but natural fibres like coir suffer from poor interfacial adhesion with polymer matrices, often necessitating chemical treatments that compromise environmental credentials. Pre-2026 research on wood modification, particularly thermal and steam treatments, demonstrates significant potential for altering hygroscopicity and surface chemistry without harsh chemicals. Applying these principles to non-wood fibres like coir could overcome a key barrier to their performance in durable, moisture-resistant applications such as external cladding.
proposals.hypothesis: A sequential physical-mechanical treatment protocol—combining steam explosion, low-oxygen thermal modification, and plasma activation—will sufficiently modify coir fibre chemistry and topography to achieve interfacial adhesion and composite performance comparable to chemically treated benchmarks, while improving long-term moisture stability.
proposals.method: The protocol begins with a controlled steam explosion of coir fibres to disrupt hemicellulose and increase porosity. This is followed by a low-oxygen thermal treatment (180–220°C) to further reduce hydrophilicity, drawing on established wood thermal modification knowledge. A subsequent atmospheric plasma treatment will activate the fibre surface. Modified fibres will be compounded with a bio-based resin (e.g., furanic or modified lignin) and formed into composites. Performance will be validated through dynamic vapour sorption analysis, mechanical testing (tensile, flexural), and accelerated weathering, with comparisons to untreated and silane-treated controls.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a robust, low-chemical pathway for valorising coir fibres in high-performance composites, directly contributing to the field of sustainable construction materials. It will provide new insights into translating wood modification science to non-wood lignocellulosics and generate fundamental data on the moisture sorption behaviour and durability of physically modified coir composites.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the hierarchical integration of physical and mechanical treatments—specifically the sequence of steam explosion and thermal modification derived from wood science—applied to coir, and the systematic evaluation of its bio-compatibilization effect without relying on traditional chemical coupling agents.

proposals.title: Enhancing Coir-Wood Composite Performance through Physical Fibre Modification: A Study of Hygroscopic Behaviour and Durability
proposals.motivation: The development of sustainable bio-composites for construction is critical for reducing environmental impact. However, reliance on chemical treatments for natural fibre modification raises concerns about toxicity, cost, and end-of-life recyclability. Coir fibre, a durable and moisture-resistant agricultural by-product, presents a promising alternative to wood fibres, but its effective use in composites without chemical coupling agents remains a significant, unexplored challenge in materials science.
proposals.hypothesis: That targeted physical and mechanical treatments of coir fibres—specifically steam explosion and high-shear mechanical fibrillation—can sufficiently modify fibre morphology and surface properties to achieve strong interfacial bonding with a wood-derived matrix, resulting in composites with competitive mechanical performance and improved moisture sorption behaviour compared to untreated composites.
proposals.method: Coir fibres will undergo two sequential physical treatments: (1) steam explosion under controlled pressure (1.5-2.5 MPa) and temperature (180-220°C) to induce micro-fracturing and increase surface area, followed by (2) high-shear mechanical fibrillation in a refiner. The modified fibres will be compounded with a thermally modified wood flour matrix using a twin-screw extruder to fabricate composite samples. Key evaluations will include mechanical testing (tensile, flexural), dynamic vapour sorption (DVS) analysis to model hygroscopic behaviour, and accelerated fungal decay tests (EN 113) to assess durability without biocides.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, chemically benign protocol for developing high-performance coir-wood composites, directly contributing to the field of green material engineering. It will generate fundamental data on the moisture sorption kinetics and long-term service life of such physically modified biocomposites, offering critical insights for their use in external building applications like cladding.
proposals.novelty: This approach challenges the prevailing pre-2026 paradigm that chemical modification is essential for adequate fibre-matrix adhesion in natural fibre composites. It uniquely integrates Dr. Hill's expertise in wood modification and archaeological wood conservation—particularly understanding moisture behaviour in degraded cellular structures—to solve an inverse problem: creating durable composites from inherently durable but difficult-to-bond fibres.

proposals.title: A Diagnostic Framework for Process-Property Optimization of Coir-Polymer Composites in Low-Energy Manufacturing
proposals.motivation: Coconut coir is an abundant, low-cost lignocellulosic fiber with potential for sustainable composites, but its adoption in high-volume, low-energy processes like injection moulding is limited by poor thermal stability, weak fiber-matrix adhesion, and process-induced damage. Overcoming these barriers is critical for reducing the embodied energy of biocomposites and expanding their use in the built environment, aligning with global sustainability goals. Current research often focuses on single modification techniques without a systematic analysis of failure modes under specific processing conditions.
proposals.hypothesis: The failure of coir composites in low-energy processes is not inherent but results from specific, diagnosable incompatibilities between fiber properties and process dynamics, which can be resolved through a targeted, adaptive engineering of both material formulation and processing parameters.
proposals.method: The research will employ a diagnostic framework adapted from wood science methodologies for analyzing moisture sorption and thermal degradation. Coir fibers will be characterized for thermal stability (TGA), surface chemistry (FTIR), and hygroscopicity. Composite failure modes (e.g., fiber pull-out, charring, fiber breakage) will be systematically mapped against injection moulding parameters (temperature, pressure, screw speed) using designed experiments. Targeted interventions—including fiber treatments (alkali, acetylation, thermal modification inspired by wood technology) and coupling agents—will be applied based on diagnostic outcomes. Process parameters will then be adaptively optimized using response surface methodology to create a compatible system.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a transferable, systematic methodology for diagnosing and solving processability issues in natural fiber composites, moving beyond trial-and-error. It will deliver a specifically engineered coir-composite formulation and process window viable for low-energy injection moulding, contributing to the life cycle assessment of sustainable building materials.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in applying a diagnostic, failure-mode-led framework from wood modification and archaeological conservation science to the process engineering of a non-wood fiber, treating the manufacturing process not as a fixed constraint but as an adaptable variable within a material-system design.

proposals.title: Leveraging Sorption Hysteresis Mechanisms to Define Low-Energy Processing Windows for Dimensionally Stable Coir Composites
proposals.motivation: The manufacturing of durable biocomposites from coir, a resilient lignocellulosic waste, is hindered by the high energy demands of hot-pressing. Concurrently, the fundamental molecular mechanism behind sorption hysteresis in wood and other plant materials remains a critical, unresolved question in materials science. This research addresses both challenges by using the established sorption models from wood science to engineer a novel, low-energy processing route for coir composites.
proposals.hypothesis: The sorption hysteresis observed in wood provides a mechanistic model for the irreversible, energy-dissipative polymer interactions in coir during processing; actively controlling this hysteresis via specific temperature-moisture-pressure pathways will 'lock' the composite into a stable, low-swelling morphology akin to that achieved through high-energy densification.
proposals.method: We will first characterize the sorption hysteresis of coir fibres using dynamic vapour sorption (DVS) coupled with in-situ FTIR spectroscopy to map hydrogen bonding rearrangements and polymer swelling states. This data will inform the development of a processing window map, correlating fibre moisture content, glass transition temperature (Tg) of lignin-hemicellulose matrix, and applied pressure. Controlled injection molding trials will then be conducted within defined windows, with composite stability assessed via water soaking, cyclic humidity tests, and microscopy to evaluate morphology 'locking'.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, low-energy manufacturing protocol for stable coir composites, directly contributing to sustainable materials engineering. It will also provide a definitive, experimentally-grounded molecular explanation for sorption hysteresis in a complex lignocellulosic system, advancing fundamental wood and plant science.
proposals.novelty: The fusion of fundamental wood sorption theory with composite process engineering is non-obvious; it inverts the typical view of hysteresis as a material flaw to be avoided, instead treating it as a controllable mechanism for achieving stability. The targeted application of this mechanism to coir processing is entirely new.

proposals.title: Engineering Coir as a Hydroscopic Buffer: Analysing and Optimising Moisture Sorption Kinetics for Functional Biocomposites
proposals.motivation: Current material science often seeks to reduce hydrophilicity in natural fibres like coir to improve dimensional stability, potentially discarding a valuable functional property. In agriculture, high water-holding capacity materials are prized for buffering soil moisture. Translating this principle to engineered materials could enable passive humidity regulation in buildings and packaging, addressing energy use and product degradation. This aligns with the researcher's expertise in wood moisture behaviour and the conservation of hygroscopic materials.
proposals.hypothesis: That the inherent, high hygroscopicity of coir fibre can be systematically engineered into biocomposites to create predictable, passive moisture-buffering systems, where the kinetics of water sorption and desorption can be tuned for specific environmental performance targets.
proposals.method: The research will characterise the dynamic vapour sorption (DVS) behaviour of raw and physically/chemically modified coir fibres to establish baseline isotherms and kinetic models. Coir will be incorporated into composite matrices (e.g., bio-based polymers, cementitious binders) at varying loadings and orientations. The moisture buffer value (MBV) of composites will be measured using established Nordtest protocols. Sorption kinetics will be modelled using the parallel exponential kinetics (PEK) model and Fickian diffusion principles to correlate microstructure with performance.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a fundamental framework for designing with, rather than against, natural fibre hydrophilicity, establishing coir as a functional component for moisture management. It will deliver predictive models linking coir composite processing to sorption kinetics, offering new material solutions for passive climate control and conservation applications.
proposals.novelty: The approach fundamentally reframes coir's hydrophilicity from a drawback to a designable asset, applying soil science concepts of moisture buffering to engineered biocomposites—a direct analogy not previously explored in material science for this fibre.

proposals.title: Beyond the Fibre Loading Plateau: A Multi-Objective Framework for Optimising Wood-Polymer Composite Performance and Processability
proposals.motivation: Wood-polymer composites (WPCs) are critical for sustainable construction, yet their design is often governed by a simplistic maximisation of fibre loading to improve stiffness and reduce cost. This overlooks complex, non-monotonic trade-offs with fracture toughness, moisture sorption behaviour, and manufacturability (e.g., void formation), leading to suboptimal material selection for specific service environments, such as external cladding or components requiring damage tolerance. A systematic, data-driven framework is needed to balance these competing objectives, informed by principles from wood modification and archaeological wood conservation which highlight the profound influence of microstructure and hygro-expansion on long-term performance.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesise that for a given wood fibre-polymer matrix system, an intermediate or 'sub-maximal' fibre loading exists that delivers a Pareto-optimal balance of mechanical properties, environmental stability, and processing viability, and that this optimum shifts predictably based on target application profiles.
proposals.method: A designed experiment will fabricate WPCs across a full spectrum of fibre loadings (e.g., 10-70 wt.%). Characterisation will include dynamic vapour sorption (DVS) isotherms, flexural and tensile properties, fracture toughness (K_IC), and quantitative void analysis via X-ray micro-computed tomography. Processing parameters (e.g., extrusion torque, melt viscosity) will be recorded. Response surface methodology and multi-objective optimisation algorithms (specifically the Non-dominated Sorting Genetic Algorithm II, NSGA-II) will be used to model the relationships and identify Pareto fronts for distinct application scenarios (e.g., high durability vs. high-volume manufacture).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a predictive, quantitative framework for WPC design, moving from a one-dimensional 'more fibre is better' paradigm to a multi-criteria selection tool. It will deliver specific processing windows and optimal formulations for targeted performance profiles, directly applicable to the building and construction sector. The integration of sorption and defect analysis with mechanical data will offer new insights into the hygro-mechanical failure modes of biocomposites.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in treating fibre loading not as a variable to be maximised, but as a multi-objective optimisation problem where a lower loading can be superior, and in explicitly linking processing-induced defects (voids) and hygroscopic behaviour to the composite's fracture mechanics within this optimisation framework.

proposals.title: Synergistic Optimization of Coir Fibre Loading and Injection Moulding Processability for Enhanced Wood-Plastic Composite Performance
proposals.motivation: The drive for sustainable, bio-based composites has increased the use of coir fibres in wood-plastic composites (WPCs). However, research often prioritizes maximizing fibre loading for static mechanical properties, typically using hot-pressing, which is not scalable for complex parts. This neglects the critical processability constraints of high-volume manufacturing methods like injection moulding, where high fibre content leads to poor flow, fibre degradation, and inconsistent part quality, ultimately limiting industrial adoption.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that for injection-moulded coir-polypropylene composites, there exists a fibre loading below the typical maximum that optimizes the synergy between composite mechanical performance and injection moulding processability, resulting in superior overall part quality and consistency compared to high-loading formulations.
proposals.method: The research will employ a full factorial design, manufacturing composites with coir loadings from 10% to 40% by weight in polypropylene with a maleated coupling agent. Processability will be quantitatively assessed using a spiral flow mould to measure flow length and pressure drop, and by analysing fibre length distribution post-processing via optical microscopy. Mechanical performance (tensile, flexural, impact) will be evaluated according to ASTM standards. Data will be analysed using response surface methodology to identify the loading that maximizes a combined performance-processability metric.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a material formulation guideline specifically tailored for the injection moulding process, moving beyond a simple property-maximization approach. It will establish a framework for balancing material and processing parameters in natural fibre composites, contributing to the scalable, energy-efficient manufacturing of high-quality WPCs. The findings will be directly applicable to industries seeking to replace glass fibres with sustainable alternatives without sacrificing manufacturability.
proposals.novelty: This study challenges the prevailing 'more fibre is better' paradigm by explicitly prioritizing the manufacturability requirements of injection moulding, a gap in coir composite literature which is dominated by hot-press studies. It fuses material science with polymer processing engineering to find a process-specific optimum, rather than a generic material one.

proposals.title: Partitioning Variance in Natural Fibre Composites: A Process-Chain Sensitivity Analysis of Coir Fibre Variability
proposals.motivation: The use of natural fibres like coir in composites is limited by their inherent biological variability, which leads to inconsistent final product performance. Current research often examines fibre properties or processing steps in isolation, failing to model their complex interactions within a full manufacturing chain. This gap hinders the reliable industrial adoption of these sustainable materials in construction and other sectors.
proposals.hypothesis: The total variance in the mechanical properties of a coir fibre composite is not dominated by initial fibre variability alone, but by specific, critical interactions between fibre batch characteristics and key processing parameters, particularly chemical treatment and drying.
proposals.method: A full factorial experimental design will be employed, tracking distinct coir fibre batches (varied by source, maturity, and extraction method) through a defined process chain: alkaline treatment, controlled drying, compounding with a bio-based polymer (e.g., PLA), and hot-press molding. The mechanical properties (tensile, flexural) of the resulting composites will be measured. A multivariate Analysis of Variance (ANOVA) and a sensitivity analysis (e.g., using Sobol indices) will partition the total variance in these properties to quantify the relative contribution of each fibre variable and process parameter.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a quantitative model identifying the most critical control points in a composite manufacturing chain for mitigating natural fibre variability. It will deliver actionable guidelines for process optimisation to achieve consistent performance, directly supporting quality assurance for bio-based building materials. The methodological framework will be transferable to other variable natural resources.
proposals.novelty: Unlike studies focusing on isolated factors, this research adopts a holistic, process-chain perspective to deconvolute the sources of variance. It applies formal sensitivity analysis, common in engineering design, to the specific problem of biological material inconsistency in composite manufacturing.

proposals.title: A Time-Temperature-Moisture Superposition Framework for Predicting Long-Term Durability of Modified Wood Composites
proposals.motivation: Predicting the long-term performance of wood composites and modified timber in exterior applications, such as cladding, is a major challenge. Current weathering tests are time-consuming and often fail to accurately model the complex, synergistic degradation caused by decades of UV exposure, moisture sorption, and thermal cycling. This creates uncertainty in material specification and life-cycle assessment for sustainable construction.
proposals.hypothesis: That the principles of pharmaceutical accelerated stability testing can be adapted to create a controlled, multi-stress aging protocol for wood composites, and that the resulting degradation data can be modelled using time-temperature-moisture superposition to reliably predict long-term material behaviour.
proposals.method: We will develop a factorial experimental design exposing modified wood and wood-polymer composite samples to combined cycles of UV radiation (using a xenon-arc weatherometer), elevated temperature (40-70°C), and controlled humidity (65-95% RH), informed by ICH Q1A(R2) stability guidelines. Degradation will be monitored via mass change, modulus of elasticity (MOE) loss, colour change, and FTIR spectroscopy. The data will be analysed using time-temperature-moisture superposition principles, employing the Arrhenius equation and Williams-Landel-Ferry (WLF) type models to shift data and construct master curves predicting property loss over decadal timescales.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, accelerated methodology for assessing wood composite durability, directly linking short-term laboratory tests to long-term field performance. It will deliver predictive models that enable specifiers and engineers to forecast service life and degradation pathways, thereby supporting the reliable use of sustainable timber products in building envelopes.
proposals.novelty: The explicit cross-disciplinary analogy with pharmaceutical stability testing protocols and the application of time-temperature-moisture superposition models to the complex degradation of bio-based composites represent a significant advance over standard, single-stress weathering tests.

proposals.title: Long-term Performance of Modified Wood Composites: A Study of Mechanical Degradation and Hygroscopic Behaviour Under Simulated Environmental Cycling
proposals.motivation: The use of modified wood and wood composites in external applications, such as cladding, is increasing, but their long-term durability remains inadequately quantified. Pre-2026 research, including reviews on thermal modification and cladding performance, highlights a critical knowledge gap regarding how these materials age under combined environmental stresses of moisture sorption and thermal fluctuation, which is essential for predicting service life and ensuring sustainable construction.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesise that the long-term mechanical integrity and dimensional stability of modified wood composites under environmental exposure are governed by the degradation of the wood-polymer interface and irreversible changes in the cell wall sorption behaviour induced by the initial treatment.
proposals.method: The study will employ a factorial design, exposing samples of thermally modified wood and wood-polymer composites to accelerated weathering cycles (UV, water spray, and freezing) simulating decades of service. Mechanical properties (modulus of rupture, hardness) will be tracked periodically. Moisture sorption isotherms will be generated at intervals using dynamic vapour sorption (DVS) apparatus to quantify hygroscopicity changes. Data will be analysed using time-series regression and principal component analysis to correlate property loss with sorption hysteresis and chemical changes (FTIR spectroscopy) over time.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a quantitative, mechanistic framework for predicting the service life of modified wood composites in building envelopes. It will directly inform standards for durability testing and material selection, bridging the gap between wood modification science, archaeological conservation insights into long-term degradation, and practical building cladding performance.
proposals.novelty: While aging studies exist for solid wood, this project uniquely integrates long-term mechanical testing with detailed sorption kinetics analysis for modern modified composites, applying methodologies from archaeological wood conservation to understand degradation pathways in contemporary engineered materials.

proposals.title: A Materials Informatics Framework for the Predictive Design of Coir Hybrid Composites with Targeted Hygro-Mechanical Performance
proposals.motivation: The demand for sustainable, bio-based building materials is growing, but the development of natural fibre composites remains largely empirical, leading to inefficient use of resources and unpredictable long-term performance. Coir fibre, while abundant and resilient, often exhibits high moisture sorption and variable properties, limiting its reliable application in external environments. A data-driven approach is needed to systematically design hybrid composites that mitigate these weaknesses and meet specific engineering requirements.
proposals.hypothesis: That a machine learning model trained on a curated database of natural fibre properties and composite performance can accurately predict, and subsequently inverse-design, coir-based hybrid composite formulations with optimized hygro-mechanical properties for targeted service life conditions.
proposals.method: A comprehensive database will be constructed from literature and new experimental data, capturing fibre properties (e.g., aspect ratio, lignin content), treatment methods, matrix types, and resulting composite properties (tensile strength, modulus, moisture sorption isotherms). Gradient Boosting Machines (e.g., XGBoost) and Multi-Layer Perceptron neural networks will be trained to predict composite performance from formulation inputs. These predictive models will then be inverted using optimization algorithms (e.g., genetic algorithms) to recommend fibre combinations, ratios, and treatments for user-defined property targets. Recommended designs will be fabricated, subjected to controlled moisture cycling, and mechanically tested for validation.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, closed-loop informatics framework that transitions the design of natural fibre composites from trial-and-error to a predictive science. It will provide specific, validated formulations for durable coir hybrids, directly applicable to non-structural building components like cladding. The resulting open database and models will serve as a foundational tool for future sustainable materials development.
proposals.novelty: While materials informatics is established for metals and polymers, its application to the complex, hygroscopic systems of natural fibre composites is nascent. This proposal uniquely integrates sorption behaviour and durability considerations—core to the researcher's expertise—into the predictive modelling and inverse design loop, moving beyond static mechanical property prediction.

proposals.title: Influence of Polymer Matrix Selection on the Hygro-Mechanical Performance of Modified Wood Fibre Composites
proposals.motivation: Wood-polymer composites (WPCs) are increasingly used in construction and cladding, but their long-term durability is critically dependent on moisture sorption behaviour, a key theme in Dr. Hill's research. While fibre modification (e.g., thermal treatment) is known to improve dimensional stability, its interaction with different polymer matrices beyond commonly studied polypropylene is poorly understood. This knowledge gap limits the optimisation of WPCs for specific service environments, particularly where moisture exposure is high.
proposals.hypothesis: The efficacy of wood fibre modification treatments (e.g., thermal, acetylation) in enhancing composite durability is not universal but is contingent upon the chemical compatibility and interfacial adhesion with the specific polymer matrix. We hypothesise that polar, bio-based matrices (e.g., polylactic acid) will synergise differently with modified fibres than non-polar, conventional thermoplastics, leading to distinct moisture sorption and mechanical property profiles.
proposals.method: Wood fibres from a single softwood source will be subjected to standardised thermal modification and acetylation protocols. These treated fibres, alongside unmodified controls, will be compounded at a fixed loading (40 wt%) with three distinct matrices: non-polar polypropylene (PP), polar polyethylene terephthalate (PET), and bio-based polylactic acid (PLA). Composite panels will be manufactured via twin-screw extrusion and compression moulding. The primary evaluation will consist of dynamic vapour sorption (DVS) analysis to model moisture behaviour, coupled with static and dynamic mechanical analysis (DMA) before and after accelerated water soaking cycles to assess property retention.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a systematic, data-driven framework for selecting polymer matrices paired with fibre modifications to target specific performance outcomes in WPCs, particularly for moisture-prone applications. It will directly extend the principles of wood modification and conservation science into the composite materials domain, offering new strategies for enhancing the service life of bio-based building materials.
proposals.novelty: While studies exist on fibre treatment or matrix variation in isolation, this proposal uniquely isolates the matrix variable to directly test its role in mediating the benefits of established wood modification techniques, applying a rigorous, conservation-science-informed analysis of moisture sorption to composite performance.

proposals.title: A Pharmacoeconomic Analogy for Wood-Based Composites: A Framework for Performance-Adjusted Lifecycle Cost (PALC) Assessment
proposals.motivation: The adoption of advanced wood composites and modified timber in construction and manufacturing is hindered by simplistic cost comparisons that ignore long-term performance benefits. Pre-2026, robust economic frameworks for quantifying the lifecycle value of material durability, hygroscopic stability, and reduced maintenance are lacking. This gap prevents objective, value-based decision-making for sustainable materials against conventional alternatives.
proposals.hypothesis: Adapting the Incremental Cost-Effectiveness Ratio (ICER) from pharmacoeconomics to create a Performance-Adjusted Lifecycle Cost (PALC) ratio will provide a standardized, quantitative metric for the economic viability of wood composites. This framework will demonstrate that composites with higher initial costs can show superior lifecycle value when performance benefits like moisture stability and longevity are monetized.
proposals.method: The research will adapt the Health Technology Assessment (HTA) structure. For a target application (e.g., external cladding), it will define a 'Performance-Adjusted Service Year' (PASY) metric, analogous to a Quality-Adjusted Life Year (QALY), incorporating quantified performance data (e.g., sorption behaviour, decay resistance from thermal modification). A lifecycle cost model will be built, integrating data from durability studies and LCA databases (pre-2026). The core algorithm will calculate the PALC ratio: (Incremental Cost of wood composite vs. reference material) / (Incremental PASYs generated). Sensitivity analysis will test key parameters.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the materials science and timber engineering community with a novel, standardized economic decision-support tool. It will shift the discourse from cost-per-unit to value-over-time, facilitating the market entry of high-performance, sustainable wood products. The framework will be directly applicable to assessing modified wood and archaeological wood conservation strategies.
proposals.novelty: While lifecycle costing exists, the direct methodological translation of pharmacoeconomic ICER frameworks to composite material assessment is unprecedented. It introduces a formalized 'performance-adjusted' unit of benefit, moving beyond simple physical property comparison to an integrated economic-performance metric grounded in HTA precedent.

proposals.title: Linking Nanoscale Water States to Macro-Scale Economics: A Predictive Model for Optimizing Wood-Polymer Composite Service Life and Cost
proposals.motivation: The long-term performance and economic viability of wood-polymer composites (WPCs) are critically undermined by moisture-induced degradation, including swelling, fungal attack, and loss of mechanical properties. Current material development relies on empirical, macro-scale testing, creating a costly and inefficient gap between fundamental material science and commercial application. This project directly addresses this gap by establishing a quantitative, predictive link between the nanoscale sorption behavior of water within the wood cell wall and the full life-cycle cost of WPCs in specific applications, enabling targeted engineering for durability.
proposals.hypothesis: The economic service life of a wood-polymer composite for a given application can be predicted by quantifying the states and dynamics of water within its wood component's nanopores, as these parameters dictate the kinetics of hygroscopic stress and biological susceptibility.
proposals.method: We will employ low-field nuclear magnetic resonance (LF-NMR) relaxometry and dynamic vapour sorption (DVS) to characterize water states (bound vs. free) and sorption hysteresis in a matrix of industrially relevant WPCs with varying polymer matrices, wood fractions, and modification treatments (e.g., thermal modification). These nanoscale data will be integrated with accelerated aging tests to develop a regression model predicting macro-scale property decay. This predictive model will then be coupled with a discounted cash flow analysis framework, using Python-based stochastic modeling to optimize composite formulation against target cost-performance thresholds for defined applications like exterior decking.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, quantitative framework for material scientists and engineers to design WPCs based on first-principles hygroscopic behavior rather than trial-and-error. It will deliver a predictive tool that links fundamental material properties to economic outcomes, allowing manufacturers to make data-driven decisions to penetrate specific market segments. The methodology bridges the fields of wood science, polymer engineering, and techno-economic analysis.
proposals.novelty: While nanoscale moisture analysis and WPC development exist separately, their direct integration with a formal economic optimization model is novel. The approach moves beyond correlating moisture content with properties to using the fundamental *state* of water within the cell wall as the primary predictor for service life cost, a concept informed by conservation science but not applied to composite engineering.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Contribution of Lignin, Cellulose, and Hemicellulose to the Sorption Enthalpy of Modified and Archaeological Wood
proposals.motivation: The heat of sorption is a fundamental thermodynamic property governing wood's moisture behaviour, crucial for dimensional stability and durability in construction and conservation. While thermal and chemical modifications are known to alter sorption, the specific contribution of each wood polymer (lignin, cellulose, hemicellulose) to the overall sorption enthalpy remains poorly quantified. This gap limits the predictive modelling of wood's hygroscopic response in both modern engineered timber and conserved archaeological wood.
proposals.hypothesis: The measured net isosteric heat of sorption for a wood sample can be expressed as a weighted sum of the contributions from its lignin, cellulose, and hemicellulose components, with the weighting factors altered predictably by modification processes such as thermal treatment.
proposals.method: We will use a suite of wood samples with controlled chemical composition, including thermally modified wood (to reduce hemicellulose content), delignified wood, and model archaeological samples. Sorption isotherms will be measured gravimetrically at multiple temperatures (e.g., 25°C, 35°C, 45°C) using dynamic vapour sorption (DVS) to calculate the net isosteric heat of sorption via the Clausius-Clapeyron equation. Chemical composition will be determined using standard wet chemistry methods (e.g., TAPPI standards for Klason lignin and sugar analysis for polysaccharides). A multivariate regression model (e.g., Partial Least Squares Regression) will be employed to correlate the sorption heat data at specific moisture contents with the quantified mass fractions of the three primary polymers.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative framework linking sorption thermodynamics directly to wood's chemical composition. It will deliver coefficients describing the specific sorption enthalpy of each polymer component, enabling better prediction of moisture behaviour in modified wood products. For conservation science, it will offer a thermodynamic basis for understanding the altered hygroscopicity of degraded archaeological wood.
proposals.novelty: While sorption heats are measured and polymer composition is analysed separately, directly regressing one against the other to assign enthalpy contributions is novel. Applying this approach across a spectrum from modified modern wood to degraded archaeological wood creates a unified explanatory model not previously attempted.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Exothermic Desorption of Water from Modified and Archaeological Wood: Implications for Drying Energy and Material Stability
proposals.motivation: The heat of sorption during moisture uptake in wood is well-studied, but its thermodynamic counterpart—the energy released during drying (desorption)—remains poorly quantified. This knowledge gap is critical for understanding the full energy balance in wood drying processes, which impacts industrial efficiency, and for assessing the thermal stresses imposed on water-sensitive materials like archaeological wood during conservation treatments. A systematic study of desorption enthalpy is needed to complete the hygrothermal picture of wood-water interactions.
proposals.hypothesis: The enthalpy of desorption for water in wood is significant and varies systematically with wood type (e.g., thermally modified vs. unmodified) and moisture history, and that this released heat can influence the local microenvironment and stress state of the material during drying.
proposals.method: We will employ isothermal calorimetry, specifically a TAM Air microcalorimeter, to directly measure the heat flow during controlled desorption steps. Samples of Scots pine (Pinus sylvestris), both unmodified and thermally modified (Thermowood), alongside archaeological oak (Quercus spp.) replicates, will be preconditioned to specific relative humidities. The calorimetric data will be coupled with dynamic vapour sorption (DVS) measurements to correlate heat release with precise mass loss. Data analysis will involve integrating heat flow curves to calculate total enthalpy and modelling the results using the Clausius-Clapeyron relation and Hailwood-Horrobin sorption theory.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive dataset on the exothermic desorption of water from wood, directly linking material science with process engineering. It will establish a fundamental parameter for energy-efficient kiln scheduling and offer new insights into the potential for thermally driven damage during the drying of heritage objects, thereby bridging the fields of wood modification and archaeological conservation.
proposals.novelty: While sorption heat is a standard measurement, the explicit focus on the desorption phase as a source of measurable, material-dependent heat release is novel. Applying this precise calorimetric approach to contrast modified, unmodified, and model archaeological wood creates a unique comparative framework not found in pre-2026 literature.

ideas.title: Moisture-Mediated Optimization of Wood Fibre Loading in Composites
ideas.description: This research investigates the hypothesis that the optimal fibre loading in wood-polymer composites is dynamically dependent on environmental moisture content. By precisely quantifying how water plasticization lowers the glass transition temperature (Tg) of the wood fibre surface and the adjacent polymer matrix, we can model the resulting changes in interfacial stress transfer and composite toughness. This allows for the identification of a 'performance envelope' where a lower fibre loading under wet conditions may provide equivalent or superior mechanical performance to a higher loading under dry conditions, optimizing for both material efficiency and economic cost. The outcome is a predictive framework for tailoring fibre content to specific climatic service environments, moving beyond the static 'more fibre is better' paradigm.

ideas.title: Economic Optimization of Wood Composites via In Situ Nanoscale Moisture Monitoring
ideas.description: This research direction develops a framework to directly link the fundamental, nanoscale behavior of water within wood cell walls to the macro-scale economic viability of wood-polymer composites. By integrating novel experimental methods to quantify water states in nanopores with application-specific cost-benefit analysis, it enables the creation of predictive models for material performance degradation and maintenance costs. This allows for the economic optimization of composite formulations and treatments based on their measured hygroscopic performance at the molecular level. Consequently, manufacturers can make data-driven decisions to target specific market applications (e.g., exterior decking, automotive interiors) with composites engineered for optimal cost-performance over their entire service life.

ideas.title: Predictive Manufacturing of Engineered Wood: Linking Hot-Press Parameters to Long-Term Hygro-Mechanical Performance
ideas.description: This research fuses process optimization with performance prediction by systematically investigating how hot-press parameters (temperature, pressure, time) alter the fundamental sorption properties and internal stress state of the wood-adhesive bond line. The resulting data will be used to parameterize and validate a multi-scale hygro-mechanical model that predicts moisture-induced stresses and deformations in full-scale glulam or CLT elements. Consequently, the optimal manufacturing conditions are defined as those that minimize long-term performance risks from moisture cycling, creating a direct link from factory settings to engineered reliability in structures.

ideas.title: Quantifying the Glass Transition in Wet Wood via Polymer Blend Plasticization Models
ideas.description: This idea expands the original seed by drawing an analogy from synthetic polymer science, where the plasticization of polymer blends by diluents is well-quantified. The analogous solution uses thermodynamic models (e.g., Gordon-Taylor) to predict the depression of the glass transition temperature based on composition. Adapted to wet wood, the research would precisely measure how water plasticization affects the glass transition temperature of wood's amorphous polymers. By treating wood as a natural polymer blend and water as a plasticizer, one can apply and potentially modify these established models to quantify the interaction, providing a predictive framework for how moisture content mechanistically softens wood at a molecular level, with applications in wood processing, durability, and composite design.

ideas.title: Transfer fiber treatment methods from wood composites
ideas.description: Apply chemical treatments used for wood fibers to modify flax fiber-matrix adhesion.
ideas.sourceTitle: Deformation and fracture behaviour of flax fibre reinforced thermosetting polymer matrix composites

ideas.title: Adapting Composite Material Homogenization to Model Moisture Effects in Engineered Wood
ideas.description: This research adapts the solution to a parallel scaling problem in composite materials science, where micromechanical models are homogenized to predict macroscopic behavior. The analogous field is Composite Materials Science, where homogenization techniques are used to upscale from fiber/matrix interactions to predict the anisotropic properties of large-scale structures. The analogous solution involves modeling fundamental micromechanics and using mathematical upscaling to account for defects and environmental effects. This is adapted to the context of engineered wood by treating wood laminae and adhesive as composite constituents. The adapted idea is to model the fundamental sorption mechanisms at the lamina level, then develop a multi-scale computational homogenization framework to upscale these local moisture effects. This will predict macroscopic warping, stress distributions, and long-term creep in large-scale glued laminated timber or CLT, providing a physics-based tool for design and durability assessment under real-world hygrothermal conditions.

ideas.title: Adapting Pharmacoeconomic Frameworks for Composite Material Application Viability
ideas.description: This idea adapts the rigorous, standardized cost-effectiveness frameworks used in pharmacoeconomics and Health Technology Assessment (HTA) to the problem of assessing the economic viability of advanced composite materials in product applications. In pharmacoeconomics, new drugs are evaluated not just on price but through metrics like the Incremental Cost-Effectiveness Ratio (ICER), which balances cost against quantified health benefits (e.g., Quality-Adjusted Life Years). The adapted research would develop an analogous 'Performance-Adjusted Lifecycle Cost' framework for composites. For a target application (e.g., automotive body panels, wind turbine blades), the analysis would quantify all incremental costs (materials, manufacturing, tooling) and model the monetized benefits over the product's lifecycle—such as fuel savings from weight reduction, reduced maintenance from corrosion resistance, or increased energy capture from improved performance. The output would be a standardized viability ratio, enabling objective, comparative economic assessments across different composite applications and material systems, moving beyond simple cost comparisons to true value-for-money analyses.

ideas.title: Hierarchical Physical-Mechanical Bio-Compatibilization for High-Performance Coir Composites
ideas.description: This research develops a robust protocol for creating high-performance coir composites without traditional chemical treatments by addressing interfacial weakness through a multi-scale physical and mechanical strategy. It employs a sequence of steam explosion and low-oxygen thermal treatment to modify fibre chemistry physically, followed by plasma exposure to activate the surface. The composite design incorporates bio-based resin matrices with molecular structures designed for physical interlocking and uses mechanically textured fibres to enhance mechanical adhesion. The system is validated by comparing the durability, moisture resistance, and mechanical properties of the resulting composites against both untreated and chemically treated benchmarks.

ideas.title: Model moisture sorption based on hydroxyl availability

ideas.title: Optimizing Coir Fibre Loading for High-Quality Injection Moulding
ideas.description: This research investigates the existence of an optimal, potentially lower, coir fibre loading specifically for the injection moulding process, challenging the assumption that higher loading always yields better composites. It aims to determine the fibre content that balances mechanical performance (e.g., strength, stiffness) with critical injection moulding processability factors like flow length, fibre degradation, and mould filling, which are compromised at high loadings. The outcome will be a process-specific material formulation guideline that enables the manufacture of high-quality coir composites using the lower-energy, scalable injection moulding method, rather than the energy-intensive hot press. This synergy optimizes for both economic and mechanical performance by tailoring the material for the process, rather than forcing a process to accommodate a maximized material property.

ideas.title: A Continuum Mechanics Framework for Moisture-Dependent Wood Properties: Quantifying the FSP Transition Zone
ideas.description: This research develops a predictive, multi-scale continuum model that explicitly quantifies the fiber saturation point (FSP) as a diffuse transition zone rather than a sharp boundary. The framework integrates sorption thermodynamics, polymer swelling mechanics, and microstructural heterogeneity to describe how key properties—like mechanical modulus, dimensional change, and permeability—evolve continuously across the hygroscopic range. This allows for the generation of property-specific 'apparent FSP' values and their dependence on material history, temperature, and species, providing a robust engineering tool that supersedes the single-threshold assumption.

ideas.title: Coir as a Hydroscopic Buffer: Adapting Soil Moisture Retention Strategies to Material Science
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that reduced hydrophilicity is always beneficial for coir by drawing an analogy from agriculture and soil science. In that field, materials with high water-holding capacity (like peat or vermiculite) are valued as soil amendments to buffer moisture—absorbing excess and releasing it slowly to prevent plant stress. The analogous solution is to use these hydrophilic materials strategically for their absorption properties, not to eliminate them. Adapted to this research context, the idea is to design and engineer coir-based composites where its natural hydrophilicity is the core advantage. The research would explore applications such as built-in humidity regulators for packaging, passive irrigation substrates for horticulture, or moisture-buffering layers in construction materials, systematically optimizing coir's absorption and release kinetics for specific performance goals.

ideas.title: Analyze extractive transformation in wet thermal modification

ideas.title: Multi-Scale, Data-Efficient Design of Hygro-Mechanically Optimized Wood Products
ideas.description: This research fuses multi-scale physical modeling with statistical Design of Experiments (DoE) to create an efficient, targeted optimization pipeline for wood product treatments. A molecular-to-macroscopic model of water sorption identifies key atomistic or microstructural parameters that most influence bulk hygro-expansion. A DoE framework is then used to design a minimal set of experimental treatments (e.g., chemical modifications, thermal processes) that systematically vary these critical parameters, efficiently modeling their complex interactions on the macroscopic performance. This synergy allows for the data-driven discovery of optimal treatment recipes that directly target the fundamental mechanisms of moisture interaction, accelerating the development of dimensionally stable wood composites.

ideas.title: Process-chain sensitivity analysis of coir fibre variability on composite performance
ideas.description: This research quantifies the impact of coir fibre source, maturity, and extraction variability not in isolation, but within the context of a complete composite manufacturing process. A factorial design will track distinct fibre batches through controlled sequences of chemical treatment, drying, compounding, and molding. The analysis will partition the total variance in final composite mechanical properties, determining the relative contribution of initial fibre variability versus each major processing parameter. The outcome is a validated model identifying which stages in the chain most critically require control or compensation to achieve consistent composite performance from variable natural fibres.

ideas.title: Use mercury intrusion porosimetry on modified wood

ideas.title: Molecular-Level Process Control for Low-Energy Coir Composite Manufacturing
ideas.description: This research fuses the challenge of manufacturing quality coir composites via low-energy methods with the fundamental quest to explain sorption hysteresis in lignocellulosics. It posits that the hysteresis observed in wood provides a critical model for understanding the irreversible, energy-dissipative interactions of coir's cell wall polymers with water during processing. By establishing a definitive molecular mechanism for this hysteresis—linking it to polymer swelling, hydrogen bonding network rearrangements, and glass transitions—the research will define precise processing windows (temperature, pressure, moisture content) for injection molding. The goal is to actively manage the sorption hysteresis mechanism to 'lock in' a stable composite morphology, achieving the dimensional stability and performance of hot-pressed materials through a controlled, lower-energy alternative.

ideas.title: Data-driven design of coir-based hybrid composites via materials informatics
ideas.description: This research moves beyond empirical mixing by establishing a predictive, data-driven design system for coir hybrid composites. A core database is populated with standardized properties of coir and other natural fibres (e.g., jute, hemp, flax) alongside composite performance data from literature and controlled experiments. Machine learning models are trained on this dataset to predict key composite properties (strength, modulus, moisture absorption) based on fibre combinations, ratios, treatments, and matrix type. The system then inverts these models to recommend optimal hybrid designs for target property profiles, which are subsequently fabricated and tested for validation and model refinement, creating a closed-loop design framework.

ideas.title: Accelerated Aging Protocol for Composites Using Pharmaceutical Stability Testing Principles
ideas.description: This research adapts the solution from pharmaceutical stability testing, where accelerated aging under elevated stress conditions is used to predict long-term shelf life. The analogous problem of predicting material degradation over decades is solved in pharma by exposing formulations to high temperature and humidity and modeling the results. The adapted idea applies this principle to composite materials by developing a controlled, accelerated aging protocol combining UV, thermal, and moisture stresses. Data from these accelerated tests will be used to create predictive models, such as time-temperature-moisture superposition models, to forecast the composite's long-term durability and performance loss under realistic environmental aging cycles, significantly reducing the time needed for such evaluations.

ideas.title: Long-Term Durability of Composites via Non-Capillary Hysteresis Mechanisms
ideas.description: This research investigates how long-term environmental aging and mechanical degradation of treated composites are governed by sorption hysteresis mechanisms beyond traditional capillary condensation. It posits that molecular-scale phenomena—such as polymer chain relaxation, irreversible chemisorption, or nanoconfinement effects in the composite matrix—are the primary drivers of hysteresis and thus control the accumulation of moisture damage over decades. By experimentally decoupling these mechanisms from macro-pore effects, the work aims to develop predictive models for service life that are based on fundamental material physics rather than empirical correlations, ultimately enabling the design of composites with intrinsically superior and more durable water resistance.

ideas.title: Quantifying Cooperative Sorption in Wood: A Multi-Scale Model Integrating Site Interaction and Heterogeneity
ideas.description: This research develops a predictive, multi-scale model for water sorption in wood that explicitly rejects the independent site assumption. It integrates atomistic simulations to parameterize cooperative sorption mechanisms into a statistical mechanics lattice model incorporating site heterogeneity and nearest-neighbor interactions. The model's predictions for hysteresis, sorption isotherms, and swelling strain are validated against high-resolution experimental data from dynamic vapor sorption and moisture-conditioned X-ray tomography. The final output is a quantifiable framework that demonstrates superior predictive power over classical Langmuir-type models for specific engineering applications like durability assessment.

ideas.title: Diagnostic and Adaptive Process Engineering for Coir Composites
ideas.description: This research systematically identifies and solves the specific material science barriers that cause coir composites to fail in low-energy, high-volume processes like injection moulding. Instead of declaring one method as uniquely viable, it employs a diagnostic framework to analyze failure modes—such as fiber-matrix adhesion, thermal stability, and flow-induced fiber damage—under alternative processing conditions. The approach then adapts both the material formulation (e.g., via targeted fiber treatments or coupling agents) and the process parameters to create a compatible system. The goal is to engineer a viable, lower-energy manufacturing pathway by directly addressing the root causes of failure, not by excluding methods a priori.

ideas.title: Wood as a Model Hierarchical System: Developing Next-Generation Hydrated Polymer Composite Physics
ideas.description: This project uses the wood cell wall's natural hierarchy as a model system to develop new physical frameworks for the dynamics of hydrated polymer composites, moving beyond simplistic synthetic polymer analogies. It combines targeted experimental techniques that can probe component-specific transitions (e.g., lignin softening, hemicellulose mobility) with computational models that explicitly account for the mechanical percolation of the cellulose network. The core outcome will be a predictive, multi-scale model that defines how a reinforcing nanofiber network constrains and modifies the glass transition behavior of a plasticized amorphous matrix. This fundamental understanding, derived from wood, will then be tested and applied back to guide the design of advanced synthetic biocomposites and materials.

ideas.title: Multi-Objective Optimization of Fibre Loading for Balanced Performance, Processability, and Defect Tolerance
ideas.description: This research systematically investigates the non-monotonic relationships between fibre loading and a comprehensive set of outcomes, moving beyond a simple strength-cost trade-off. It employs a designed experiment to fabricate composites across a wide loading range, characterizing not only stiffness and strength but also fracture toughness, void content, and processing parameters. Data-driven models will identify optimal loadings for specific application profiles, such as damage tolerance or high-volume manufacturability. The outcome is a predictive framework that defines when and why a lower fibre loading delivers superior overall performance by avoiding property-regime-specific failure modes.

ideas.title: Adapting NMR Cryoporometry from Mesoporous Materials to Quantify Water in Wood Cell Wall Nanopores
ideas.description: This research idea adapts the established materials science technique of NMR cryoporometry to the problem of directly measuring water states in wood cell wall nanopores. In the analogous field, NMR cryoporometry is a standard method for determining pore size distributions in synthetic mesoporous materials by measuring the freezing/melting point depression of a confined probe liquid. The adapted solution involves applying this method to hydrated wood samples, using water itself as the probe. By performing variable-temperature 1H NMR experiments and analyzing the signal from unfrozen water as a function of temperature, the method can quantify the volume of water in pores of specific sizes (approximately 2-50 nm) and differentiate between strongly bound surface water and capillary water. This provides a direct, thermodynamic measurement of the cell wall's nano-porous network and the water it contains, which is critical for understanding wood-moisture relations, dimensional stability, and degradation processes.

ideas.title: Mechanism-based predictive modeling of composite durability through in-situ sensor-integrated aging
ideas.description: This research integrates optical fiber sensors and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy electrodes directly into the composite laminate to monitor in-situ the initiation of key degradation mechanisms (e.g., resin hydrolysis, fiber-matrix debonding, microcracking) during accelerated environmental exposure. The system subjects instrumented samples to combined cyclic thermal, moisture, and mechanical stress, correlating real-time sensor data with periodic ex-situ mechanical and microstructural analysis. A physics-informed machine learning model is developed to link these early-stage mechanistic signals to long-term property decay, ultimately creating a validated predictive framework for composite service lifetime under defined environmental spectra.

ideas.title: Molecularly-Programmed Interphases for Hysteresis-Controlled Wood-Polymer Composites
ideas.description: This research direction investigates the design of novel sizing agents for wood fibers that are informed by the molecular mechanisms of moisture sorption hysteresis. Instead of seeking only maximum adhesion, the chemical architecture of the sizing will be tailored to interact with the specific hydroxyl groups and nanopores responsible for hysteresis, aiming to either mitigate, stabilize, or exploit the hysteretic moisture uptake. This creates a molecularly programmed interphase that controls both the fiber-matrix bond and the local moisture environment at the interface. The ultimate goal is to synthesize wood-polymer composites whose dimensional stability and mechanical performance are predictably regulated by the engineered hysteretic behavior of the fiber interphase, leading to superior and reliable properties in hygroscopic conditions.

ideas.title: Spatially and Compositionally Resolved Mapping of Water Plasticization on Wood Polymer Transitions
ideas.description: This research deconvolves the glass transition behavior of wood by directly addressing its inherent heterogeneity. It combines spatially resolved dynamic nanoindentation with in-situ humidity control to map local viscoelastic transitions within the wood cell wall microstructure. These mechanical maps are correlated with thermal and dielectric analyses performed on isolated polymer fractions and controlled composites, assigning specific transition signatures to cellulose, hemicellulose, and lignin. The integrated multi-modal dataset provides a robust, phase-separated quantification of how water plasticizes each polymer network, moving beyond a single, misleading bulk measurement.

ideas.title: Dynamic Scenario Modeling for Composite Adoption Under Technological and Market Uncertainty
ideas.description: This research develops a computational modeling framework to assess the economic viability of advanced composites under deep uncertainty. Instead of a single cost-benefit snapshot, it constructs multiple future scenarios varying key parameters like carbon fiber price, manufacturing throughput, carbon tax policies, and competitor material evolution. The core of the system is an integrated model that runs probabilistic simulations to generate a distribution of potential outcomes, identifying critical cost thresholds and adoption tipping points. The final output is an interactive decision-support tool that quantifies risk and resilience, enabling stakeholders to test strategic investments against a range of possible futures rather than a single forecast.

ideas.title: Decipher Hysteresis in Wood Sorption via Free Energy Landscapes from Enhanced Sampling Molecular Dynamics
ideas.description: This research integrates advanced molecular dynamics (MD) simulation techniques, specifically enhanced sampling methods like metadynamics or umbrella sampling, with the core problem of sorption hysteresis in wood. It aims to simulate the complete adsorption-desorption cycle for water in nanoscale models of the wood polymer matrix (cellulose, hemicellulose, and lignin), explicitly calculating the free energy landscapes along relevant collective variables. This approach will identify and quantify the molecular-scale energy barriers—such as polymer chain relaxation, hydrogen bond network reformation, and capillary condensation in nanopores—that create the hysteresis loop. The outcome will be a first-principles, mechanistic model that can predict hysteresis magnitude based on wood composition and microstructure, fundamentally bridging the gap between molecular interactions and macroscopic material behavior.

ideas.title: Quantifying Hydrophilic Advantage: Directly Measuring Water Dynamics in Coir Cell Wall Nanopores for Engineered Performance
ideas.description: This research direction fuses the concept of beneficial hydrophilicity with direct nanoscale water measurement to fundamentally understand and engineer coir fibers. We will apply advanced experimental methods (e.g., low-field NMR, differential scanning calorimetry) to directly quantify the amount, state, and mobility of water within the nanopores of untreated coir's cell wall. This data will be explicitly correlated with performance in target applications where water absorption is advantageous, such as soil moisture retention in horticulture or humidity buffering in building materials. The synergy enables moving from qualitative claims of coir's 'good' hydrophilicity to a predictive, microstructure-based model for tailoring its water-handling properties for specific functional applications.

ideas.title: Nanoscale Water Dynamics as a Probe for Optimizing Chemical Treatments of Coir Fibers
ideas.description: This research direction applies small-angle neutron scattering (SANS) to investigate how different chemical treatments (e.g., silanes, alkali, acetylation) alter the nanoscale water distribution and porosity within coir fibers. By quantitatively mapping water accessibility to the cellulose microfibrils and lignin matrix after each treatment, the study establishes a direct link between treatment-induced nanostructural changes and the measured mechanical adhesion in a composite matrix. This approach moves beyond empirical testing by using water as a neutron-scattering probe to fundamentally understand which treatments most effectively modify the fiber's internal surface chemistry and morphology for optimal bonding. The resulting data will create a predictive framework for selecting or designing chemical treatments that target specific, adhesion-critical nanostructural features in natural fibers.

ideas.title: Probing coir-water interactions and fibre heterogeneity via NMR relaxometry
ideas.description: This research applies nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) relaxometry, a technique from soft matter physics, to systematically investigate the relationship between coir fibre provenance (source, maturity, extraction method) and the nanoscale mobility of water within the fibre structure. By correlating NMR relaxation time distributions with traditional measures of fibre and composite properties, the study will establish a direct, non-destructive probe of the intrinsic material heterogeneity that Idea A seeks to understand. This enables a fundamental, physics-based classification of coir fibres based on their interaction with moisture, predicting composite performance more reliably than bulk averaging. The synergy lies in using water as a molecular probe to decode the material variability that Idea B's methods were not designed for, creating a new diagnostic tool for natural fibre engineering.

ideas.title: Applying a Materials Genome Approach to Coir Fibre Variability
ideas.description: This research challenges the assumption of coir fibre homogeneity by adapting the solution to a parallel problem in metallurgy: managing variability in alloy properties. The analogous field is Metallurgy and Alloy Design, where the Materials Genome Initiative (MGI) framework solves variability by establishing explicit processing-structure-property (PSP) linkages through systematic experimentation and modeling. The adapted idea applies this PSP framework to coir fibres, treating source, maturity, and extraction method as input parameters. The research will involve creating a comprehensive database that links these coir 'genotype' and processing parameters to the fibre's structural 'phenotype' (e.g., cellulose crystallinity, microfibril angle, defect density) and ultimate composite performance. This data-driven approach will enable predictive modeling to pinpoint the dominant sources of variability and guide the selection and processing of coir for consistent, tailored composite properties.
