ideas.title: Plasma-Tailored Coir Fibres as Nanostructural Probes via Inverse Sorption Analysis
ideas.description: This research direction integrates plasma surface modification of coir fibres with inverse analysis of sorption hysteresis data. The core novelty is using sequential, controlled plasma treatments (e.g., varying gas chemistry, power, duration) to progressively alter the surface energy and chemistry of the fibre cell walls. After each treatment step, detailed water vapour sorption isotherms are measured, and the hysteresis data is used to inversely calculate the nanopore size distribution. This allows for the direct correlation of specific plasma parameters with quantifiable changes in the cell wall's accessible pore network, moving beyond mere hydrophilicity assessment to actively engineering and diagnosing the nano-porous architecture for targeted applications like moisture buffering or composite interfaces.

ideas.title: Optimal Fibre Loading as a Nutrient-Dose-Response Problem
ideas.description: This research adapts the dose-response framework from pharmacology to challenge the assumption that higher fibre loading always improves composite properties. In pharmacology, an optimal dose exists where benefit is maximized and toxicity minimized, forming a biphasic response curve. The analogous solution involves systematic testing across a concentration range to identify this optimum. Adapted to composite materials, the idea is to experimentally map mechanical and economic performance against fibre loading to generate full response curves. The research will seek to identify a potential optimum loading—a 'composite therapeutic window'—where specific property enhancements are most cost-effective, recognizing that excessive fibre can cause 'toxic' effects like agglomeration and porosity that degrade performance, thereby establishing that an optimal, often lower-than-maximum, fibre loading exists for targeted applications.

ideas.title: Adapting Atmospheric Plasma Jet Protocols from Medical Device Sterilization to Coir Fibre Treatment
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from medical device surface functionalization to the treatment of natural coir fibres. In the analogous field, atmospheric-pressure plasma jets (APPJs) are used to sterilize and functionalize heat-sensitive polymer surfaces for medical implants by precisely controlling gas chemistry and exposure to graft specific functional groups. The adapted research idea applies this APPJ methodology to coir fibres, systematically using different reactive gas admixtures (like argon-oxygen or argon-ammonia) and treatment parameters to selectively modify the fibre surface chemistry. The objective is to move beyond generic plasma cleaning to achieve a reproducible, targeted grafting of functional groups (e.g., carbonyl, amine) that will chemically enhance the interfacial adhesion between the fibre and polymer matrices in composite materials, thereby improving mechanical performance.

This integrated treatment-and-encapsulation system ensures the modified fibre surface is preserved until composite fabrication, yielding a robust and durable interface with predictable, long-term adhesion properties.

By using SANS to map water location and movement within the composite's nanostructure—specifically at fiber-matrix interfaces and between different fiber types—the work will uncover the fundamental mechanisms governing hygroscopic stress, swelling, and property degradation. This direct, nanoscale visualization enables the rational design of hybrid composites where fiber selection and treatment are optimized not just for mechanical reinforcement, but for targeted moisture management and long-term environmental stability.

ideas.title: A Multi-Scale, Damage-Aware Computational Framework for Predicting Composite Failure
ideas.description: This research develops a predictive framework that overcomes the limitations of analytical micromechanics by explicitly simulating damage evolution at the micro-scale. A computational RVE, incorporating realistic microstructures and calibrated constituent properties, is subjected to virtual mechanical tests to capture progressive failure mechanisms. The extracted damage-dependent effective properties and failure envelopes are used to inform and calibrate a macroscopic continuum damage model. This approach provides robust, physics-based predictions of composite strength and post-elastic behavior, directly addressing the gap between idealized theory and experimental failure observations.

ideas.title: Investigate single wood polymer chain hydration using single-molecule force spectroscopy and microfluidic isolation
ideas.description: This idea adapts a core methodology from biophysics—single-molecule force spectroscopy—to solve the challenge of studying isolated wood polymer chain hydration. In biophysics, the analogous problem of understanding how individual biomolecules interact with their solvent environment is solved by isolating and mechanically probing single polymer chains (e.g., DNA, proteins) using techniques like AFM and optical tweezers within controlled microfluidic environments. The adapted research idea applies this solution by proposing to tether a single cellulose or hemicellulose chain, extracted or synthesized, within a humidity-controlled microfluidic cell. Using AFM-based force spectroscopy, the research would directly measure the force-extension behavior, hydration-induced swelling, and water-binding energy of the isolated chain, providing foundational data on wood polymer-water interactions at the molecular scale, decoupled from the complex composite structure of the cell wall.

ideas.title: NMR relaxometry of water dynamics on isolated wood polymer chains to deconvolute composite behavior
ideas.description: This research direction applies nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) relaxometry—specifically T1 and T2 relaxation time measurements—to study water dynamics on isolated, well-characterized hemicellulose and cellulose chains in controlled aqueous environments. By systematically varying polymer chain length, substitution, and conformation, the approach quantifies how each polymer's intrinsic chemical and structural features dictate the rotational and translational mobility of bound and free water. The resulting dataset provides a foundational 'library' of NMR relaxation signatures for individual wood polymers, which can then be used to deconvolute and interpret the complex, averaged relaxometry data obtained from intact wood, moving from phenomenological observation to mechanistic, chemistry-driven prediction.

ideas.title: Closed-loop calibration of a predictive sorption model using direct nanoscale water observations
ideas.description: This research direction integrates sorption-based inverse modeling with direct experimental quantification of water in nanopores to create a validated predictive model. Direct measurements (e.g., via NMR cryoporometry or DSC thermoporosimetry) of water states during sorption cycles provide ground-truth data on pore filling/emptying. This data is used to calibrate and constrain the inverse calculation from sorption hysteresis, significantly improving its accuracy and physical realism. The resulting closed-loop framework enables the deduction of not just pore size distribution, but also insights into pore connectivity, surface chemistry effects, and the mechanisms behind sorption hysteresis in the complex wood cell wall matrix.

ideas.title: Atomistic Simulation of Water Cluster Nucleation and Dynamics on Isolated Wood Polymer Chains
ideas.description: This research direction employs advanced ab initio molecular dynamics (AIMD) and enhanced sampling techniques to simulate, at the atomic scale, how water molecules sequentially assemble into defined clusters on specific sites of a single, isolated wood polymer chain. It moves beyond modeling bulk-like water behavior to reveal how the chain's precise conformation, functional group spacing, and stereoelectronic properties act as a template, directing the critical first steps of hydration. The outcome will be a fundamental mechanistic understanding of the primary hydration sites, cluster stability, and hydrogen-bond network dynamics that seed larger-scale moisture uptake in wood materials.

ideas.title: A Composite Micromechanics Framework for Cell Walls with Non-Aqueous Hydration Phases
ideas.description: This research direction fuses the reconceptualization of cell wall water as a structured, non-bulk phase with the formal mathematics of composite micromechanics. It treats the cellulose microfibrils as the reinforcing fibres and the hydrated pectin/hemicellulose matrix not as a simple polymer solution, but as a complex, multi-phase material whose properties (stiffness, viscosity, swelling pressure) are dictated by the constrained, ordered state of its water. By applying models like Mori-Tanaka or self-consistent schemes, the framework will predict macroscopic wall mechanics (elastic modulus, hygro-expansion) from first principles based on the volume fraction and arrangement of cellulose and the explicitly modeled properties of the structured aqueous matrix. This enables the rigorous testing of hypotheses about water's role and provides a quantitative bridge between nanoscale polymer-water interactions and whole-cell/organ biomechanics.

ideas.title: Apply Effective Medium Theory from Geophysics to Predict Composite Properties
ideas.description: This idea adapts solution strategies from geophysics to the problem of predicting composite material properties. In geophysics, effective medium theories (e.g., Self-Consistent, Differential Effective Medium) are standard tools for calculating the bulk seismic properties of a rock from the properties and fractions of its mineral grains and pore fluids. By directly analogizing the fibre-matrix system in composites to the mineral-fluid system in rocks, these established mathematical averaging schemes can be adapted. The adapted research idea involves applying the formalisms of these geophysical effective medium models to micromechanical modeling. The solution entails using the known elastic properties, volume fractions, and geometric descriptors (aspect ratio, orientation) of fibres and matrix as inputs to these averaging schemes to compute the anisotropic effective stiffness tensor of the composite, thereby providing a predictive bridge from constituent data to macroscopic mechanical behavior.

ideas.title: Quantifying the Anomalous Thermodynamics and Dynamics of Cell Wall Water Under Mechanical Stress
ideas.description: This research tests the hypothesis that cell wall water exhibits quantifiable deviations from bulk aqueous solution behavior due to nanoconfinement and direct coupling to the stressed polymer matrix. Using a model plant tissue under controlled osmotic and mechanical stress, we employ in situ NMR to measure water diffusion coefficients, relaxation times, and spectral densities as a function of applied wall tension. The core innovation is correlating these dynamic water properties directly with the mechanical state of the wall, seeking to identify non-linear, stress-dependent relationships that violate predictions for a simple polymer solution. This provides a physical, falsifiable framework to replace the qualitative 'not a simple solution' claim.

ideas.title: Closed-loop multiscale framework for wood hygro-expansion using machine-learned scale-bridging
ideas.description: This research develops a robust, data-driven framework to predict moisture-induced swelling in wood by directly linking molecular structure to bulk deformation. Instead of relying on traditional, error-prone analytical upscaling, the core innovation is a machine-learned model trained on a large dataset generated from atomistic simulations of wood polymer-water interactions under varied conditions. This model directly predicts continuum-scale strain fields from molecular configuration and humidity input, creating a more reliable scale bridge. The framework is closed-loop, with targeted macroscopic swelling experiments continuously validating and refining the machine learning model to ensure predictive accuracy and physical consistency.

ideas.title: Adapting Response Surface Methodology from Pharmacology for Chemical Treatment Optimization
ideas.description: This idea adapts Response Surface Methodology (RSM), a well-established optimization technique from pharmacology and drug development, to the problem of optimizing chemical treatment duration and concentration for material property enhancement. In the analogous field, RSM is used to model the complex, often non-linear interactions between multiple formulation and process variables (like drug concentration and processing time) to find optimal combinations that maximize desired outcomes. The adapted research idea involves designing a Central Composite Design or similar experimental array that systematically varies treatment time and chemical concentration as interacting factors. The resulting property data (e.g., strength, corrosion resistance) is used to fit a second-order polynomial model, generating a predictive response surface. This approach efficiently identifies the global optimum parameter set, quantifies the interaction effect between time and concentration, and provides a robust model for predicting treatment outcomes, offering a more sophisticated and resource-efficient solution than conventional one-variable-at-a-time or full-factorial grid searches.

ideas.title: Adapting Adsorption Site Mapping from Catalysis to Biomass Water Sorption
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from heterogeneous catalysis, where molecular dynamics and electronic structure methods are used to map gas adsorption sites on catalyst surfaces. The analogous problem of understanding site-specific interactions is applied to biomass-water systems. The adapted research idea uses molecular dynamics simulation to map the heterogeneous 'adsorption sites' for water on hemicellulose and cellulose surfaces, identifying specific functional groups and structural features that govern binding energy, water cluster formation, and the overall sorption dynamics. This provides a mechanistic, atomistic picture of moisture uptake in biomass, analogous to how catalyst-adsorbate interactions are modeled.

ideas.title: Modeling Water Sorption in Wood as a Cooperative, Heterogeneous Network
ideas.description: This idea challenges the Langmuir-type assumption of homogeneous, non-interacting sorption sites for water in wood by drawing an analogy from biochemistry's solution to the problem of non-independent binding sites. In protein-ligand binding, the Monod-Wyman-Changeux allosteric model successfully describes how binding sites are not independent; the binding of a ligand induces a conformational shift that changes the affinity of other sites in a cooperative manner. Adapted to wood science, the research idea is to model the wood's hygroscopic polymer matrix as a heterogeneous, cooperative network. The sorption of a water molecule at one site (e.g., a hydroxyl group on hemicellulose) is hypothesized to strain or rearrange local polymer chains, thereby altering the chemical potential and binding energy of adjacent sites. This approach predicts sorption isotherms and hysteresis loops based on the coupled, state-dependent behavior of the entire sorbent network, moving beyond the summation of identical, independent sites to a more physically realistic framework of interacting sites.

ideas.title: Quantum-Accurate Machine Learning Molecular Dynamics for Water Sorption in Plant Cell Wall Polymers
ideas.description: This research overcomes the force field inaccuracy limitation by employing a machine learning-driven molecular dynamics framework. The system is first trained on a targeted dataset of hemicellulose, cellulose, and water clusters, with energies and forces computed using high-level Density Functional Theory. The resulting neural network potential then drives extended molecular dynamics simulations, capturing the subtle polarization and hydrogen-bonding dynamics critical for sorption. This approach provides predictive accuracy for binding energies, diffusion barriers, and sorption isotherms that are directly validated against available experimental spectroscopic and gravimetric data.

ideas.title: Biomimetic Surface Priming for Composite Adhesion
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that chemical treatment is necessary for good composite properties by drawing an analogy from orthopedic implant biointegration. In that field, physical surface modifications like sandblasting and acid-etching create topographies that promote bone adhesion without relying solely on bioactive coatings. The adapted research idea is to apply advanced physical patterning techniques (e.g., ultrafast laser ablation, plasma etching) directly to reinforcing fibres to create controlled micro- and nano-scale surface topographies. This biomimetic 'priming' aims to enhance mechanical interlocking with the polymer matrix, providing a novel, potentially more durable and environmentally benign pathway to superior fibre-matrix adhesion compared to traditional chemical sizing agents.

ideas.title: Bayesian fusion of sorption hysteresis and scattering data for robust cell wall nanostructure determination
ideas.description: This redesigned approach overcomes the non-uniqueness of the inverse problem by combining sorption hysteresis isotherms with a direct nanostructural probe like SAXS. A Bayesian statistical framework integrates these datasets, treating the sorption model as a prior distribution of pore sizes and updating it with the likelihood provided by the scattering data. This method yields not just a single pore size distribution, but a full posterior distribution that explicitly quantifies the uncertainty and confidence in the result. The final output is a robust, probabilistically validated model of the cell wall's nanoporosity.

ideas.title: Robust Multi-Objective Bayesian Optimization of Chemical Treatment Under Process Noise
ideas.description: This research system employs a sequential model-based optimization strategy to efficiently navigate the complex parameter space of chemical treatment time and concentration. An initial space-filling experimental design builds a preliminary Gaussian process model linking parameters to key property outcomes. An acquisition function then guides each subsequent experiment to balance exploration and exploitation, targeting the global optimum. The core advancement is the explicit inclusion of noise variables within the experimental design phase, enabling the identification of treatment parameters that not only enhance properties but are also robust to realistic manufacturing variations, ensuring the optimal solution is practical and reliable.

ideas.title: Cell wall water as a structured hydrogel with confined dynamics
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that cell wall water behaves as a bulk aqueous solution with dissolved polymers by drawing an analogy from materials science. In polymer physics, the problem of water confined within synthetic hydrogels was solved by recognizing it as a structured composite with multiple physical states, not a simple solution. Adapting this solution, the research would reconceptualize the plant cell wall as a dynamic, charged hydrogel where water exists in distinct populations (e.g., bound, interfacial, confined mobile). The project would employ techniques like time-domain NMR, dielectric spectroscopy, and fluorescence recovery after photobleaching (FRAP) with molecular probes to map the spatial heterogeneity and restricted dynamics of water and solutes, fundamentally altering models of diffusion, signaling, and wall mechanics.

ideas.title: Hysteresis as a Metastable State Transition, Not Just Pore Filling
ideas.description: This idea inverts the assumption that sorption hysteresis is primarily caused by capillary condensation in macro-pores by drawing an analogy from phase transformations in materials science. In metallurgy, hysteresis in martensitic transformations is understood as a path-dependent transition between two distinct phases with an energy barrier, not a simple reversible process. Adapting this solution, the research proposes that sorption hysteresis stems from a first-order phase transition between metastable states of the adsorbate, where the nucleation energy for a condensed phase during adsorption differs from the energy required to destabilize it during desorption. This shifts the explanatory focus from capillary effects in pore bodies to the dynamics of phase nucleation and growth influenced by pore neck geometry and molecular-scale interactions, offering a more fundamental mechanism for hysteresis.

ideas.title: Quantifying Pore Network Connectivity and Molecular Rearrangement as the Dominant Hysteresis Mechanism in Macro-Pores
ideas.description: This research program systematically isolates and quantifies the contribution of pore network connectivity (ink-bottle effects) and associated molecular-scale rearrangements to sorption hysteresis, explicitly decoupling it from classic capillary condensation. Using a series of synthesized model materials with identical macro-pore body sizes but precisely engineered pore throat dimensions, it combines high-resolution sorption isotherms with in-situ NMR spectroscopy to track fluid connectivity and molecular mobility during adsorption and desorption. The direct correlation between throat geometry, the magnitude of hysteresis, and the onset of percolation provides a falsifiable, mechanistic alternative to the capillary condensation paradigm for macro-porous systems.

ideas.title: Adaptive Sequential Experimentation with Automated Feature Discovery
ideas.description: This system employs an adaptive, closed-loop methodology for optimization in complex, uncertain domains. It begins with a flexible, model-agnostic design to initially probe the response surface, then iteratively refines the experimental focus using Bayesian optimization principles. Crucially, it incorporates automated techniques for constructing and testing potential higher-order interactions and transformed variables, allowing the discovery of significant effects not specified in the original experimental plan. This results in a robust, data-driven approach that efficiently converges on optimal regions while learning the underlying system structure.
